# WebChat/Control-UI 图片无法上传到后端 — 2026.4.15 仍未修复

## 问题描述

在 OpenClaw 2026.4.15 版本中，通过 WebChat/Control-UI 发送图片（Ctrl+V 粘贴或点击附件按钮），图片在 UI 中显示缩略图，但实际**没有任何上传请求发出**，Gateway 无法收到附件。

Agent 收到的消息元数据中完全没有 `attachments` 字段：
```json
{
  "label": "openclaw-control-ui",
  "id": "openclaw-control-ui"
}
```

正常情况下应该包含：
```json
{
  "label": "openclaw-control-ui",
  "id": "openclaw-control-ui",
  "attachments": [
    {
      "path": "~/.openclaw/workspace/media/inbound/xxx.png",
      "type": "image/png",
      "filename": "screenshot.png"
    }
  ]
}
```

## 复现步骤

1. 打开 Control-UI (http://127.0.0.1:18789/)
2. Ctrl+V 粘贴截图，或点击 📎 附件按钮选择图片
3. 图片在输入框中显示缩略图
4. 发送消息
5. 观察：图片消失，但 Gateway 日志无任何 media/attachment 相关记录
6. Agent 收到的元数据无 `attachments` 字段

## 环境

- OpenClaw 版本: 2026.4.14 / 2026.4.15
- 操作系统: macOS 25.4.0 (arm64) — Mac Mini
- 浏览器: Safari / Chrome（均复现）
- 安装方式: npm global install
- 主模型: bailian/qwen3.6-plus

## 对比

| 渠道 | 图片支持 | 状态 |
|------|---------|------|
| 飞书 | ✅ | 正常工作 |
| Telegram | ✅ | 正常工作 |
| WebChat/Control-UI | ❌ | 图片无法传输 |

## 浏览器 DevTools 观察

- Network 面板中**没有任何图片上传请求**（无 POST/fetch/XHR）
- 只有 `blob:` 本地预览（200 OK），图片停留在浏览器内存中
- 存在用于文本消息的长连接 WebSocket/SSE
- 刷新页面后，之前"发送"的图片消失

## 相关 Issue

- #53271 — WebChat/Control-UI does not upload images
- #50775 — webchat Channel Attachment Image Path Not Injected
- #52787 — Image upload in Control UI webchat not working
- #30818 — Web UI chat: Pasted images not sent to backend
- #46534 — 据称已修复（但实际未修复）
- #51487 — Feature Request: Support paste image directly into webchat input box

## 影响

- WebChat 完全无法用于多模态工作流
- 用户必须切换到其他渠道（飞书/Telegram）才能发图片
- 严重影响使用体验

items.title: Bug: Subagent deliveryContext does not inherit parent group session — falls back to DM
QQ Bot cannot send images saved to media/outbound (Media path must be inside QQ Bot media storage)
items.body:
## Bug: QQ Bot cannot send locally-generated images

### Description

When an AI agent (e.g., kimi-for-coding) takes a screenshot or generates an image, it saves the file to `~/.openclaw/media/outbound/`. However, when the QQ Bot channel tries to send this image, it fails with:

```
sendMedia(auto) error for C:\Users\...\.openclaw\media\outbound\xxx.png: Media path must be inside QQ Bot media storage
```

### Root Cause

In `dist/extensions/qqbot/outbound-*.js`, the `resolveQQBotPayloadLocalFilePath()` function validates local file paths against `allowedRoots`, which only includes `getQQBotMediaDir()` (i.e., `~/.openclaw/media/qqbot/`). Files in `media/outbound/` or `media/inbound/` are rejected.

The relevant code:
```js
const allowedRoots = [getQQBotMediaDir()];
for (const root of allowedRoots) {
    const resolvedRoot = path.resolve(root);
    if (isPathWithinRoot(canonicalCandidate, fs.realpathSync(resolvedRoot))) return canonicalCandidate;
}
```

Meanwhile, the `resolveOutboundMediaPath()` function does support `options.extraLocalRoots`, but the caller (image sending path) doesn't pass `outbound` or `inbound` as extra roots.

### Impact

- QQ Bot users cannot receive screenshots taken by the agent
- QQ Bot users cannot receive any locally-generated images that are saved to `media/outbound/`
- The agent enters an infinite retry loop trying different send methods, eventually timing out (3 min+)

### Workaround

Adding `media/outbound` and `media/inbound` to `allowedRoots`:

```js
const allowedRoots = [getQQBotMediaDir(), path.join(getHomeDir(), ".openclaw", "media", "outbound"), path.join(getHomeDir(), ".openclaw", "media", "inbound")];
```

### Expected Fix

Either:
1. Add `media/outbound` and `media/inbound` to the default `allowedRoots` in `resolveQQBotPayloadLocalFilePath()`
2. Or automatically copy outbound images to `media/qqbot/` before sending
3. Or have the caller pass `extraLocalRoots` when sending images via QQ Bot

### Environment

- OpenClaw version: 2026.4.15
- OS: Windows 11
- Channel: QQ Bot
- Model: kimi-for-coding

items.title: Operator field report: config enum footgun, runtime fanout observability, and low-cost maintenance pattern
items.body:
# Field report after multi-host maintenance/dashboard setup

A practical operator report from a long homelab session with OpenClaw across multiple machines.

## Environment
- OpenClaw running in a multi-host personal setup
- Mix of Debian host plus other Linux machines
- Heavy use of Control UI, agent sessions, memory, cron-style automation, and custom operational dashboards
- Recent work involved reducing model use for recurring maintenance and simplifying the operating pattern

## Main outcomes

### 1. Config footgun caused startup failure
I hit a failure after setting an agent tool config value like:

```json
"tools.exec.host": "host"
```

In this context the valid enum was actually:

```text
auto | sandbox | gateway | node
```

The bad value caused OpenClaw startup failure.

#### Why this matters
The failure itself is defensible because strict config validation is good, but this kind of mistake is easy to make because `"host"` feels semantically valid to an operator.

#### Suggestion
If not already covered cleanly, it may be worth improving one or more of:
- error wording
- nearby docs/examples
- schema descriptions for `tools.exec.host`
- “did you mean `auto`?” style hints on invalid enum values

### 2. Large RAM pressure appeared tied more to lingering process/tool fanout than to baseline OpenClaw size
Earlier in the session, OpenClaw had been consuming dramatically more RAM, to the point it looked pathological.

After cleanup/repair work, RAM usage dropped massively, which strongly suggests the issue was not simply “OpenClaw is inherently huge”, but some combination of:
- lingering MCP/process fanout
- stuck or multiplied background sessions
- residual runtime state after experimentation/debugging

I do not yet have a minimal repro, so this is not a hard bug claim. It is an operator observation:
- when the system is healthy, RAM can be much lower than a bad episode suggests
- when it goes bad, better visibility into lingering sub-process/session/tool fanout would help a lot

#### Suggestion
Anything that improves operator visibility for:
- active child processes
- MCP server count/fanout
- persistent session/subagent leftovers
- per-surface runtime load

would help diagnose “why is memory suddenly enormous?” incidents.

### 3. A very effective low-cost ops pattern: move repetitive maintenance out of model turns
One especially successful pattern from tonight was shifting recurring daily maintenance away from LLM-driven turns and into narrow local maintenance runners driven by systemd user timers.

That gave a much cheaper and more predictable steady state:
- less token usage
- less session churn
- less “agent doing routine chores conversationally”
- easier reasoning about what runs daily and why

OpenClaw still provided the control plane, visibility, and operator interaction surface, but the recurring maintenance itself no longer depended on model turns.

#### Suggestion
This may be worth documenting as an endorsed ops pattern:
- use OpenClaw for orchestration, review, dashboards, and exceptions
- use narrow local scripts/timers for repetitive deterministic maintenance
- reserve model turns for analysis, decisions, and exceptions

### 4. Dashboard/operator UX mattered a lot
A small but meaningful UX improvement was making logs open inline in a scrollable dashboard bubble instead of just linking out to files.

That materially improved operator usability because:
- less context switching
- faster glanceability
- easier review of maintenance output

Not necessarily an OpenClaw core issue, but it reinforced how much value there is in keeping operational review surfaces compact and in-band.

## Net takeaway
My broad conclusion after this session:

- OpenClaw becomes much more pleasant in steady state when repetitive maintenance is made deterministic and local
- strict config validation is good, but some enum/value surfaces could benefit from stronger operator guidance
- high RAM episodes may sometimes reflect runtime/process fanout pathologies rather than normal baseline cost
- better observability into background runtime state would be very valuable for real operators

If helpful, this can be split later into:
- a tighter bug report on the config enum issue
- a separate issue/request on fanout/process observability
- or a docs PR around the “model-free maintenance runner” pattern

items.title: Gateway drops image attachments for custom providers despite image input configured
items.body:
## Bug Description

When sending images in chat, the Gateway drops attachments with log message:

```
parseMessageWithAttachments: 1 attachment(s) dropped — model does not support images
```

This happens even when the custom provider has `"input": ["text", "image"]` explicitly configured in `models.providers`.

## Root Cause

The Gateway has a **hardcoded provider capability whitelist** that only recognizes these providers as image-capable:

- openai, anthropic, minimax, moonshot, google, qwen, mistral, groq, deepgram

Any provider defined in `models.providers` (even if named `qwen` or `zai`) is treated as a "custom provider" and **not** included in this whitelist. The attachment is dropped at the message parsing stage, before the model is even invoked.

## Reproduction

1. Configure a custom provider in `openclaw.json`:

```json
{
  "models": {
    "providers": {
      "zai": {
        "api": "https://open.bigmodel.cn/api/paas/v4",
        "models": {
          "glm-4.6v-flash": {
            "input": ["text", "image"]
          }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}
```

2. Set primary or imageModel to use this provider
3. Send an image in chat
4. Check gateway logs: attachment is dropped

## Expected Behavior

Custom providers with `"input": ["text", "image"]` configured should be recognized as image-capable. The capability should be inferred from the configuration, not from a hardcoded whitelist.

## Workaround

Use MCP tools (e.g., glm-vision MCP server) to analyze images separately, bypassing the Gateway attachment pipeline.

## Related Issues

- #55667 - zai provider media-understanding broken since 2026.3.12
- #33185 - imageModel fails with "Unknown model" for custom providers
- #51150 - PR to resolve imageModel for custom providers (open, not yet merged)

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: latest (npm installed)
- OS: Windows Server 2019
- Custom provider: zai (BigModel/GLM)

items.title: gateway start should check for existing instance before starting
items.body:
## Issue: gateway start should check for existing instance before starting

**Problem**:
Running `openclaw gateway start` when an instance is already running will launch a second Gateway process, causing:
- CPU and memory to spike (2 instances competing)
- Potential disk exhaustion
- System instability

**Expected behavior**:
Before starting, `openclaw gateway start` should:
1. Check if port 18789 is already in use
2. Check for a lock file (e.g., `/tmp/openclaw-gateway.lock`)
3. If an existing instance is detected, refuse to start and show an error message like:
   "Gateway is already running on port 18789 (PID: XXXX). Use 'systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway' to restart."

**Workaround for now**:
- The systemd service already has `Restart=always`, so users should use `systemctl --user restart openclaw-gateway` instead of manually running `openclaw gateway start`

**Environment**:
- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)
- OpenClaw version: 2026.3.31

items.title: honcho_search_conclusions always returns empty — wrong SDK method called
items.body:
## Bug: `honcho_search_conclusions` always returns empty results

**Type:** bug  
**Labels:** bug, plugins  
**Area:** honcho memory plugin  
**Version:** OpenClaw 2026.4.15, openclaw-honcho 1.2.2

---

## Bug Description

The `honcho_search_conclusions` tool (registered by `openclaw-honcho`) **always returns "No memories found"** even when relevant conclusions exist in Honcho's database.

Root cause: `dist/tools/search.js` calls the wrong SDK method.

### Current (buggy) code — `search.js`

```javascript
const representation = await state.ownerPeer.representation({
    searchQuery: query,
    searchTopK: topK ?? 10,
    searchMaxDistance: maxDistance ?? 0.5,
});
```

`ownerPeer.representation({ searchQuery })` calls `POST /peers/{peer_id}/representation` **without a `target`** parameter. This queries "what does the `owner` peer know about themselves" — a self-conclusion scope where `observer = observed = owner`. Since the `owner` peer never draws conclusions about themselves, this always returns empty.

### Correct code

```javascript
const conclusions = await state.agentPeer
    .conclusionsOf(state.ownerPeer)
    .query(query, topK ?? 10, maxDistance ?? 0.5);
```

`agentPeer.conclusionsOf(ownerPeer)` returns a `ConclusionScope` scoped to `observer = agent-main, observed = owner` — the agent's observations about the user. `ConclusionScope.query()` calls `POST /conclusions/query` with the correct `filters.observer_id` / `filters.observed_id`, which **correctly returns results**.

### SDK reference

The Honcho TypeScript SDK (`@honcho-ai/sdk@2.1.1`) provides:
- `peer.conclusions` — self-conclusions (`observer = observed = peer`)
- `peer.conclusionsOf(target)` — scoped conclusions (`observer = peer`, `observed = target`)  
- `peer.representation(options)` — LLM-summarized representation (needs `target` for local scope)

The plugin tool description says "Find specific past context... about the user" — this requires `agent→owner` conclusions, which `conclusionsOf` provides.

---

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Install `openclaw-honcho` plugin (self-hosted, local Honcho at `localhost:8000`)
2. Ensure Honcho has captured messages and drawn conclusions (several sessions of chat)
3. Call `honcho_search_conclusions` with any query that matches existing conclusions:
   ```
   honcho_search_conclusions(query="what is the user's timezone", topK=3)
   ```
4. **Actual:** Returns `No memories found matching: "what is the user's timezone"`
5. **Expected:** Returns the relevant conclusions (e.g., timezone facts drawn from prior sessions)

## Environment

- openclaw version: **2026.4.15**
- Plugin: `@honcho-ai/openclaw-honcho@1.2.2`
- Honcho API: local self-hosted at `http://localhost:8000`
- Honcho SDK: `@honcho-ai/sdk@2.1.1`

## Impact

The `honcho_search_conclusions` tool — one of the core memory retrieval tools — is completely broken for all users. Tools like `honcho_ask` (which uses `peer.chat()`) work correctly. Only the semantic search tool is affected.

## Fix

Edit `dist/tools/search.js` in the installed plugin:

Replace the `execute` body:
```javascript
// BROKEN — queries owner's self-representation (empty):
const representation = await state.ownerPeer.representation({
    searchQuery: query,
    searchTopK: topK ?? 10,
    searchMaxDistance: maxDistance ?? 0.5,
});

// FIXED — queries agent's conclusions about the owner:
const conclusions = await state.agentPeer
    .conclusionsOf(state.ownerPeer)
    .query(query, topK ?? 10, maxDistance ?? 0.5);
```

Also update the output formatting since `ConclusionScope.query()` returns `Conclusion[]` objects (not a string):

```javascript
function formatConclusions(query, conclusions) {
    if (!conclusions.length) {
        return `No memories found matching: "${query}"...`;
    }
    return `## Search Results: "${query}"\n\n` +
        conclusions.map(c => `[${c.createdAt}] ${c.content}`).join('\n\n');
}
```

The fix is a minimal one-file change in `dist/tools/search.js`.

items.title: Support Anthropic advisor tool type (advisor_20260301)
items.body:
## Feature Request

Support the new Anthropic [advisor tool](https://claude.com/blog/the-advisor-strategy) (`advisor_20260301`) so users can pair a cost-effective executor model (Haiku/Sonnet) with Opus as an advisor.

## What it does

The advisor strategy lets a smaller model drive end-to-end and escalate hard decisions to Opus. Opus returns a plan/correction/stop signal, then the executor resumes. The advisor never calls tools or produces user-facing output.

**Results from Anthropic's benchmarks:**
- Sonnet + Opus advisor: +2.7% better than solo Sonnet, 11.9% cheaper than solo Opus
- Haiku + Opus advisor: 85% cheaper than Sonnet with strong results

## API shape

```python
tools=[
    {
        "type": "advisor_20260301",
        "name": "advisor",
        "model": "claude-opus-4-6",
        "max_uses": 3,
    },
]
```

Requires beta header: `anthropic-beta: advisor-tool-2026-03-01`

## Current limitation

`convertAnthropicTools()` in `anthropic-transport-stream.ts` hardcodes all tools as `{ name, description, input_schema }`. The advisor tool type has a different shape (`type`, `model`, `max_uses` instead of `description`/`input_schema`).

## Suggested config

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "advisor": {
        "model": "anthropic/claude-opus-4-6",
        "max_uses": 3
      }
    }
  }
}
```

When `advisor` is configured, inject the advisor tool into the tools array and add the beta header.

## Use case

Running Haiku as the primary model on Telegram/WhatsApp for cost efficiency, with Opus advisor for complex questions — best of both worlds.

items.title: [Bug]: active-memory sub-agent receives empty tools array, memory_search/memory_get never called
items.body:
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

Active-memory sub-agent's LLM request includes tools=[] instead of [memory_search, memory_get], preventing the model from calling memory tools.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Upgrade from 2026.4.14 to 2026.4.15
2. Enable active-memory plugin with any OpenAI-compatible model
3. Send a message that should trigger memory recall (e.g. "What is my favorite programming language?")
4. Check sub-agent transcript in ~/.openclaw/plugins/active-memory/transcripts/

Observed: transcript shows 6 lines only (no tool_use, no tool_result). Model's thinking says "tool not listed". Strict-validation providers like Qwen return 400: "[] is too short - 'tools'".

In 2026.4.14, the same query produces 8+ line transcripts with memory_search tool_use and tool_result entries.

### Expected behavior

In 2026.4.14, the active-memory sub-agent transcript contains tool_use (memory_search) and tool_result entries (8+ lines), and memory recall succeeds.

### Actual behavior

In 2026.4.15, the sub-agent transcript contains only 6 lines with no tool_use or tool_result. The model's thinking output says "tool not listed", confirming tools=[] was sent. 
Providers with strict validation (e.g. Qwen via OpenCode Go) reject with: 400 [] is too short - 'tools'. The plugin still reports status=ok by misinterpreting the thinking text or error message as the summary (summaryChars=56), masking the failure.

### OpenClaw version

2026.4.15 (041266a) — regression from 2026.4.14 (323493f)

### Operating system

macOS 14.7 (Sonoma)

### Install method

npm global

### Model

openrouter/openai/gpt-oss-120b:free, opencode-go/qwen3.5-plus

### Provider / routing chain

openclaw -> openrouter (gpt-oss-120b:free), openclaw -> opencode-go (qwen3.5-plus)

### Additional provider/model setup details

_No response_

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
Transcript comparison (same query: "What is my favorite programming language?"):

**2026.4.14 (working)** — 8 lines, tool call present:

L5 thinking: "Need to search memory. Use memory_search."
L5 tool_use: memory_search input={"query":"favorite programming language"}
L6 tool_result: [{path: "...", score: 0.69, snippet: "..."}]
L7 text: "User's favorite programming language is Python."


**2026.4.15 (broken)** — 6 lines, no tool call:

L5 thinking: "Need to search memory. Use memory_search tool? Not listed. Return NONE."
L5 text: "NONE"


**2026.4.15 with Qwen (strict validation)** — explicit 400 error:

L5 content: [], stopReason: "error"
L5 errorMessage: "400 [] is too short - 'tools'"


Note: plugin reports status=ok summaryChars=56 even on failure, misinterpreting thinking
text as summary.
```

items.title: Control UI doesn't render new messages dynamically — requires hard refresh
items.body:
## Summary

When sending a message via the Control UI dashboard (http://127.0.0.1:18888/), the message is submitted to the gateway successfully but does not appear in the chat message list. The assistant's response also does not render. A hard refresh (Cmd+Shift+R) of the page shows both the sent message and the response correctly.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.14 (323493f)
- Node: 24.14.1
- OS: macOS 13.7.8 (x64)
- Browser: Chrome 147.0.0.0
- Model: codex/gpt-5.4

## Steps to reproduce

1. Open the Control UI at http://127.0.0.1:18888/
2. Send a message in the chat input
3. Observe the message list

## Expected behavior

The sent message appears immediately in the chat list, followed by the assistant's streaming/final response.

## Actual behavior

The chat list does not update. The message appears to have been sent (input clears), but nothing renders. After a hard refresh, both the user message and the assistant response are visible — confirming the gateway processed them correctly.

## Notes

- Gateway logs confirm `chat.send` and `chat.final` events are emitted over the WebSocket.
- The TUI (`openclaw tui`) and CLI (`openclaw agent`) correctly display responses — the issue is isolated to the Control UI webchat Lit component.
- The `chatMessages` reactive property on the `openclaw-app` Lit element does not appear to update when new WebSocket events arrive, or the Lit render cycle is not triggered.
- The Control UI also shows persistent `token_mismatch` errors in `gateway.err.log` after gateway restarts — the browser caches a stale gateway token. This may or may not be related.

items.title: Codex harness holds session lane lock after run completes — blocks all subsequent messages
items.body:
## Summary

When using the `codex` provider plugin (Codex CLI app-server via stdio), the session lane lock is never released after a run completes. This means the first message in a session gets a response, but every subsequent message queues indefinitely behind the stale lock.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.14 (323493f)
- Node: 24.14.1
- OS: macOS 13.7.8 (x64)
- Codex CLI: @openai/codex v0.121.0
- Model: codex/gpt-5.4
- Auth: OAuth profile (ChatGPT Plus subscription)

## Steps to reproduce

1. Configure `codex/gpt-5.4` as the default model with the codex plugin enabled
2. Start the gateway (`openclaw gateway` or LaunchAgent)
3. Open the TUI (`openclaw tui`) or send a message via `openclaw agent --agent main --message "hello"`
4. Wait for the response (arrives in ~30-40s)
5. Send a second message

## Expected behavior

Second message is dispatched to the agent and a response is returned.

## Actual behavior

Second message enters the session lane queue and waits indefinitely. Gateway logs show:

```
lane wait exceeded: lane=session:agent:main:main waitedMs=291543 queueAhead=0
```

`queueAhead=0` confirms nothing is ahead — the lane itself is locked by the previous run.

`ps aux | grep codex` shows the Codex app-server process remains alive after the run. Killing the stale codex process and restarting the gateway clears the lock, but the issue recurs on the very next run.

## Effective request path

```
TUI/Control UI → WebSocket → Gateway (ws://127.0.0.1:18888) → Agent "main" → Codex Plugin (harness) → Codex CLI app-server (stdio) → OpenAI API (https://api.openai.com/v1/responses) → gpt-5.4
```

## Workaround

Kill stale codex processes and restart the gateway between messages:

```bash
kill <stale_codex_pid>
openclaw gateway restart
```

This is obviously not a viable workaround for normal use.

## Notes

- The `openclaw agent --agent main --message "..."` CLI command completes and returns a response, but the underlying harness run never signals termination to the lane manager.
- The session lane shows `waitedMs` climbing continuously with `queueAhead=0`.
- This does not occur with the `openai-codex` provider (which has separate DNS/403 issues) — it is specific to the `codex` plugin harness route.

items.title: Proposal: Improved Backup Design with Configurable Exclusion Rules and Service-Aware Archiving
items.body:
## Summary

Propose an improved backup architecture for OpenClaw that addresses current issues with file locking, symlink handling, exclusion rule configurability, and backup size optimization.

## Problem Statement

The current `openclaw backup create` command has several known issues:

- **#67417**: Fails with ENOENT when session files are cleaned up during backup (race condition)
- **#57515**: Duplicate path errors when workspace contains symlinks
- **#64144**: No way to exclude node_modules or other large directories from extensions
- **No configurable exclusion rules** — users cannot customize what gets backed up
- **No service awareness** — gateway remains running during backup, causing SQLite locks

## Proposed Architecture

### 1. Service-Aware Backup Lifecycle

```
1. Detect locked files (SQLite, WAL files)
2. If locked → openclaw gateway stop
3. Create tar.gz archive
4. Restore → openclaw gateway start
5. If user rejects service control → fall back to best-effort with warning
```

This solves #67417 by eliminating the race condition between backup enumeration and session cleanup.

### 2. Configurable Exclusion Rules

Exclusion rules should be stored in a config file, not hardcoded:

```json
{
  "excluded_extensions": ["log", "tmp", "pyc", "bak", "swp"],
  "excluded_folders": [
    "node_modules", ".git", "__pycache__", ".venv",
    "extensions", "browser", "delivery-queue", "flows",
    "skills-backup"
  ],
  "excluded_files": [".DS_Store", "Thumbs.db"]
}
```

**Why this matters**: Every OpenClaw installation is different. Some users have 100+ skills, some run in environments with `node_modules` or browser caches. Without configurability, backups either include gigabytes of rebuildable data or exclude things users actually need.

### 3. Symlink-Aware Traversal

Skip symbolic links and junction points entirely using `FileAttributes.ReparsePoint` (Windows) or `lstat` checks (Unix). This prevents:
- Duplicate paths in the archive (#57515)
- Infinite recursion through circular symlinks
- Unintentional backup of linked directories (e.g., `projects -> /d/Research/Projects`)

### 4. Output Format

Use `tar.gz` for universal compatibility:
- Zero external dependencies on most platforms
- Paths stored as absolute (e.g., `C:\Users\...\`) for disaster recovery
- Restore command: `tar -xzf backup.tar.gz`
- Compressed size typically 4-10x smaller than raw data

### 5. Two Backup Modes

| Mode | What It Includes | Typical Size | Use Case |
|------|-----------------|--------------|----------|
| `config-only` | openclaw.json, credentials, cron jobs, skills, devices | < 5 MB | Quick migration, config backup |
| `full` | Config + workspace + memory + persona files | 10-50 MB | Disaster recovery |

### Reference Implementation

I built a standalone tool implementing this design:

- **Repository**: [JIRBOY/openclaw-backup](https://github.com/JIRBOY/openclaw-backup)
- **Stack**: .NET 8, zero external dependencies (`System.Formats.Tar` + `GZipStream`)
- **Features**: Service management (gateway stop/start), UAC auto-elevation, XML-configurable exclusions, backup rotation, dry-run mode
- **Real-world results** on a typical installation:
  - Naive scan: 13,400 files, 256 MB
  - With exclusion rules: 1,995 files, 8.6 MB compressed

## Related Issues

- #67417 — ENOENT race condition (solved by service-aware backup)
- #57515 — Symlink duplicate paths (solved by symlink-aware traversal)
- #64144 — Exclude node_modules (solved by configurable exclusions)
- #26515 — Backup & Restore for settings (addressed by config-only mode)
- #13616 — Backup/restore utility (addressed by full mode)

## Impact

This would give OpenClaw users:
1. **Reliable backups** — no more ENOENT failures or locked file errors
2. **Smaller archives** — configurable exclusions prevent bloated backups
3. **Cross-platform restore** — tar.gz works everywhere
4. **Disaster recovery confidence** — knowing that a single archive can restore the entire setup

items.title: [Bug]: DM session context missing outbound bot messages even when userToken is configured
items.body:
# [Bug]: DM session context missing outbound bot messages even when userToken is configured — slack-plugin / session

---

## Summary

When a Slack user token (`xoxp-...`) is configured with `userTokenReadOnly: true`, OpenClaw does not use it to hydrate DM session history before processing an inbound reply. As a result, the agent only sees inbound messages in the session context — outbound messages the bot sent proactively (via `openclaw message send`) are absent. This causes the agent to lose context of what it originally asked when a user replies to a bot-initiated DM.

---

## Environment

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| **OpenClaw version** | 2026.4.11 (769908e) |
| **Commit / build** | 769908e |
| **OS** | Ubuntu 22.04 LTS (Linux 6.8.0-100-generic x86_64) |
| **Deployment context** | Bare-metal VPS, systemd service |
| **Node.js version** | v22.22.0 |
| **Affected component** | slack-plugin, session |
| **Agent(s) affected** | main |
| **Channel(s) affected** | Slack DMs (direct messages) |
| **Model/provider** | anthropic/claude-sonnet-4-6 |

---

## Preconditions

- Gateway running in Socket Mode
- `session.dmScope` set to `per-channel-peer`
- `channels.slack.userToken` configured with a valid `xoxp-...` user token
- `channels.slack.userTokenReadOnly: true`
- `channels.slack.dmHistoryLimit: 10`
- Bot has previously sent a proactive DM to a user via `openclaw message send --channel slack --target "user:<id>" --message "..."`
- User replies to that proactive DM

---

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure `channels.slack.userToken` with a valid user token that has `im:history` and `im:read` scopes.
2. Set `channels.slack.userTokenReadOnly: true`.
3. Send a proactive DM from the bot to a user: `openclaw message send --channel slack --target "user:<user-id>" --message "This is a test — reply to this."`
4. Have the user reply to that DM in Slack.
5. Observe the agent's response — it has no context of the message it sent in step 3.

---

## Expected Behavior

When `userToken` is configured and has `im:history` scope, OpenClaw should use it to fetch the full DM conversation history (including bot outbound messages) before building the agent's session context. The agent should be aware of the message it sent in step 3 when the user replies.

---

## Actual Behavior

The agent responds as if it has no context of the previous outbound message. The session context appears to contain only inbound messages (user → bot), not outbound messages (bot → user) sent via `message send`. The agent said: *"Honestly? No — I don't have active context from a previous session about a specific test in progress."*

---

## Error Messages / Logs

No error messages. The gateway log confirms the user token was loaded successfully:

```
config hot reload applied (channels.slack.userToken)
```

And the DM reply was delivered:

```
delivered reply to channel:<dm-channel-id>
```

No errors related to history fetching. The history fetch silently uses the bot token only, missing outbound bot messages.

---

## Frequency

Consistent / always reproducible when:
- A proactive DM is sent via `openclaw message send`
- The recipient replies in a new session turn

---

## Impact / Severity

**Medium** — The agent loses context of its own outbound messages in DM conversations, causing confusing responses when users reply to bot-initiated DMs. Workaround exists (manual state logging), but degrades conversational continuity.

---

## Evidence Classification

### Confirmed Facts
- [Confirmed] `userToken` is loaded: gateway log shows `config hot reload applied (channels.slack.userToken)`
- [Confirmed] The user token CAN read the full DM history via Slack API (`conversations.history` with the user token returns both inbound and outbound messages)
- [Confirmed] The agent session context does NOT include the bot's own outbound message when the user replies
- [Confirmed] `dmHistoryLimit: 10` is set; the conversation had only 3 messages — limit is not the cause

### Likely Contributing Factors
- [Likely] DM history hydration uses the bot token (`xoxb-...`), not the user token, even when `userToken` is configured
- [Likely] Slack's API returns different message sets for bot token vs. user token on `conversations.history` — bot token may not surface messages the bot sent proactively via the API

### Unknowns / Needs Verification
- [Unknown] Whether OpenClaw's history hydration code has a branch for user token on DM channels
- [Unknown] Whether this is intentional behavior (user token reserved for actions/directory reads only) or an oversight

---

## Suspected Root Cause

The DM history hydration step in the slack-plugin uses the bot token for `conversations.history` calls. Slack's bot token `im:history` scope may return a different (or subset) message list than the user token for the same DM channel — specifically missing messages the bot sent proactively via API. The user token is configured but not routed to the history hydration code path.

This is consistent with the docs stating: *"For actions/directory reads, user token can be preferred when configured."* — history hydration may not be classified as a "read" that prefers the user token.

---

## Workaround

Manually store the outbound message text in application state (e.g., `heartbeat-state.json`) when sending proactive DMs. On reply, check this state before responding to reconstruct context. Functional but brittle.

---

## Recent Changes

- `channels.slack.userToken` and `channels.slack.userTokenReadOnly: true` added to config as part of attempting to fix this issue (2026-04-17)

---

## Additional Notes

The user token successfully fetches full DM history when called directly via the Slack API — both inbound and outbound messages are visible. The capability exists at the token level; it is not being used by OpenClaw's session hydration.

Feature request: when `userToken` is configured with `im:history` scope, use it (instead of or in addition to the bot token) for DM history hydration before building session context.

---

*Report generated by Jarvis (OpenClaw bug-report skill) — 2026-04-17 UTC*

### Bug type

Crash (process/app exits or hangs)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary


After upgrading to 2026.4.15, the gateway can freeze silently mid-session with no error — webchat and WhatsApp stop responding — and only recovers when WhatsApp emits a 408 and the health monitor restarts the channel.

### Steps to reproduce

NOT_ENOUGH_INFO

### Expected behavior

Gateway remains responsive between WhatsApp disconnects, as it did before 2026.4.15.

### Actual behavior

Gateway stops processing requests mid-session. No error in logs, no crash. Last ws ⇄ res entry at 23:19:40, followed by 9 minutes of silence. Recovery only triggered by WhatsApp 408 at 23:28:30.

### OpenClaw version

2026.4.15 (041266a)

### Operating system

Windows 10 (10.0.19045 x64)

### Install method

 npm global

### Model

openai-codex/gpt-5.4

### Provider / routing chain

openclaw → ChatGPT OAuth (OpenAI Codex)

### Additional provider/model setup details

_No response_

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
23:19:40 [ws] ⇄ res ✓ node.list 5150ms  ← last normal response
← 9 minutes of silence
23:28:30 [whatsapp] Web connection closed (status 408). Retry 1/12 in 2.01s…
23:28:30 [health-monitor] [whatsapp:default] health-monitor: restarting (reason: disconnected)

```

items.title: memory-wiki bridge mode not exporting artifacts (memoryPluginState.capability undefined in CLI context)
items.body:
# OpenClaw Bug Report: memory-wiki bridge mode not exporting artifacts

**Date**: 2026-04-17  
**OpenClaw Version**: 2026.4.14 (323493f)  
**Reporter**: 王飞 (via OpenClaw agent)

---

## Issue Summary

`memory-wiki` plugin in `bridge` mode fails to export public artifacts from `memory-core` plugin, while `unsafe-local` mode works correctly. The `bridgePublicArtifactCount` is always 0 despite `memory-core` being properly configured and functioning.

---

## Environment

### System
- OS: macOS (Darwin 25.1.0 arm64)
- Node.js: v25.8.2
- OpenClaw: 2026.4.14 (323493f)

### Configuration

#### memory-core (active memory plugin)
```json
{
  "memory-core": {
    "enabled": true,
    "config": {
      "dreaming": {
        "enabled": true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

#### memory-wiki (bridge mode)
```json
{
  "memory-wiki": {
    "enabled": true,
    "config": {
      "vaultMode": "bridge",
      "vault": {
        "path": "/Users/wangfei/.openclaw/wiki/main",
        "renderMode": "obsidian"
      },
      "bridge": {
        "enabled": true,
        "readMemoryArtifacts": true,
        "indexDreamReports": true,
        "indexDailyNotes": true,
        "indexMemoryRoot": true,
        "followMemoryEvents": true
      }
    }
  }
}
```

#### Agents Configuration
- 3 agents configured: main (小菲), invest (小管), work (小芽)
- Each with separate workspace directories
- All workspaces contain valid memory files

---

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure `memory-wiki` with `vaultMode: "bridge"`
2. Ensure `memory-core` is enabled with dreaming enabled
3. Run wiki bridge import command:
   ```bash
   openclaw wiki bridge import
   ```

### Actual Result
```
Bridge import synced 0 artifacts across 0 workspaces (0 new, 0 updated, 0 unchanged, 0 removed).
Indexes not refreshed (no-import-changes).
```

`wiki status` shows:
```json
{
  "bridgePublicArtifactCount": 0,
  "warnings": [{
    "code": "bridge-artifacts-missing",
    "message": "Bridge mode is enabled but the active memory plugin is not exporting any public memory artifacts yet."
  }]
}
```

### Expected Result
Bridge import should detect and sync public artifacts from memory-core, similar to unsafe-local mode behavior.

---

## Verification & Workaround Testing

### Test 1: unsafe-local mode works
Changed configuration to:
```json
{
  "vaultMode": "unsafe-local",
  "unsafeLocal": {
    "allowPrivateMemoryCoreAccess": true,
    "paths": [
      "/Users/wangfei/.openclaw/workspace/memory",
      "/Users/wangfei/.openclaw-invest/workspace/memory",
      "/Users/wangfei/.openclaw-work/workspace/memory"
    ]
  }
}
```

Result:
```
Unsafe-local import synced 227 artifacts (227 new, 0 updated, 0 unchanged, 0 removed).
Indexes refreshed (231 files).
```

**Conclusion**: The memory files exist and are accessible. The issue is specific to bridge mode's capability API call chain.

### Test 2: memory-core is functioning
Gateway logs confirm `memory-core` is operating normally:
```
[plugins] memory-core: light dreaming staged 55 candidate(s) [workspace=/Users/wangfei/.openclaw/workspace].
[plugins] memory-core: REM dreaming wrote reflections from 2078 recent memory trace(s)
[plugins] memory-core: dreaming promotion complete (workspaces=3, candidates=0, applied=0, failed=0).
```

**Conclusion**: `memory-core` plugin is properly registered and functioning.

---

## Root Cause Analysis

Based on code inspection of `memory-wiki` and `memory-core` plugins:

### Call Chain
```
openclaw wiki bridge import
  → syncMemoryWikiBridgeSources()
    → listActiveMemoryPublicArtifacts()
      → memoryPluginState.capability?.capability.publicArtifacts?.listArtifacts()
        → memory-core: listMemoryCorePublicArtifacts()
```

### Suspected Issue
The `memoryPluginState.capability` is `undefined` when called from `wiki bridge import` context, causing the function to return an empty array:

```javascript
// From memory-state-CKpinHhR.js
async function listActiveMemoryPublicArtifacts(params) {
  return (await memoryPluginState.capability?.capability.publicArtifacts?.listArtifacts(params) ?? [])
    .map(cloneMemoryPublicArtifact)
    .toSorted(...);
}
```

### Possible Causes
1. **Capability Registration Timing**: `memoryPluginState.capability` may not be initialized when the CLI command runs
2. **Plugin Lifecycle**: The capability API might not be persisted across CLI invocations
3. **Gateway Context**: CLI commands may not have access to the same plugin state as the gateway runtime

---

## Impact

- Bridge mode is non-functional for syncing memory artifacts to wiki
- Users must use `unsafe-local` mode as a workaround, which:
  - Requires explicit path configuration
  - Bypasses the intended public artifact API abstraction
  - May have different security implications

---

## Workaround

Use `unsafe-local` mode with explicit paths:

```json
{
  "memory-wiki": {
    "config": {
      "vaultMode": "unsafe-local",
      "unsafeLocal": {
        "allowPrivateMemoryCoreAccess": true,
        "paths": [
          "/path/to/agent/workspace/memory"
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}
```

```bash
openclaw wiki unsafe-local import
```

---

## Suggested Fix

1. Ensure `memoryPluginState.capability` is properly initialized when CLI commands run
2. Add logging to diagnose why `capability` is undefined in CLI context
3. Consider adding a fallback that directly calls `listMemoryCorePublicArtifacts` if capability API fails

---

## Additional Context

- Gateway is regularly restarted (daily), ruling out initialization timing as the sole cause
- The issue persists across multiple restarts
- Both `memory-core` and `memory-wiki` plugins are bundled with OpenClaw (not third-party)

---

## Related Documentation

- https://docs.openclaw.ai/plugins/memory-wiki (Vault modes section)
- Bridge mode documented to work with active memory plugin's public artifacts

---

**Reporter Contact**: Via OpenClaw Feishu DM (ou_179a6bee00d6b80a51aceb3d143a779a)

items.title: [Bug] imageModel调用火山引擎API返回404错误
items.body:
## 问题描述

OpenClaw配置`imageModel.primary`为`volcengine-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`后，当用户发送图片时，OpenClaw内部的image工具调用火山引擎API返回**404错误**。

## 环境信息

- OpenClaw版本：2026.4.14
- 配置的imageModel：`volcengine-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro`
- 火山引擎API：`https://ark.cn-beijing.volces.com/api/coding/v3/chat/completions`

## 问题现象

### 1. OpenClaw内部调用失败

当用户发送图片时，OpenClaw的image工具返回错误：

```
<active_memory_plugin>
LLM request failed: provider rejected the request schema or tool payload.
</active_memory_plugin>
```

或404错误。

### 2. 直接调用API成功

使用Python直接调用同一个API，**完全正常**：

```python
import base64, requests

with open('image.jpg', 'rb') as f:
    img = base64.b64encode(f.read()).decode()

response = requests.post(
    'https://ark.cn-beijing.volces.com/api/coding/v3/chat/completions',
    headers={
        'Authorization': 'Bearer <API_KEY>',
        'Content-Type': 'application/json'
    },
    json={
        'model': 'doubao-seed-2.0-pro',
        'messages': [{
            'role': 'user',
            'content': [
                {'type': 'text', 'text': '描述这张图片的内容'},
                {'type': 'image_url', 'image_url': {'url': f'data:image/jpeg;base64,{img}'}}
            ]
        }],
        'max_tokens': 1000
    },
    timeout=60
)
# 状态码: 200 ✅
# 正常返回图片描述内容
```

### 3. 文本模型正常

OpenClaw内部调用火山引擎的文本模型（不带图片）完全正常，只有imageModel（多模态）失败。

## 可能原因

1. **OpenClaw内部image工具的请求格式不正确**
   - 可能使用了错误的endpoint
   - 可能请求body格式不符合火山引擎API要求

2. **模型ID映射问题**
   - OpenClaw可能使用了内部映射的模型ID，而非配置文件中指定的ID

3. **多模态请求处理逻辑有bug**
   - 图片base64编码方式不对
   - content数组格式不对

## 临时解决方案

创建了自定义image-recognition技能，绕过OpenClaw的imageModel工具，直接调用火山引擎API。

## 期望修复

1. 修复OpenClaw内部image工具对火山引擎API的调用逻辑
2. 或者提供更清晰的错误信息（当前只显示404，没有具体原因）
3. 在文档中说明各provider的imageModel兼容性

## 配置文件

```json
{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "model": {
        "primary": "baiduqianfancodingplan/glm-5"
      },
      "imageModel": {
        "primary": "volcengine-plan/doubao-seed-2.0-pro"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## 补充信息

- 用户环境：飞书平台
- 发现时间：2026-04-17
- OpenClaw运行环境：Linux 服务器

items.title: [Bug]: ACPX runtime probes for codex on every startup regardless of allowedAgents — causes EPIPE crash
items.body:
## Bug Description

The `acpx` embedded runtime backend unconditionally probes for a `codex` agent on every gateway startup, regardless of the `acp.allowedAgents` config. When the `codex` binary is not installed or broken, the probe fails and the gateway crashes with an uncaught `Error: write EPIPE` exception.

This is a **regression risk for any OpenClaw user** who has `acp.backend: "acpx"` enabled but does not have the Zed/codex infrastructure installed.

## Environment

- OpenClaw: 2026.4.15 (041266a)
- Node: v24.14.1
- OS: Ubuntu 24.04 (Linux 6.8.0-107-generic)
- acpx plugin: bundled (enabled via `plugins.entries.acpx.enabled: true`)
- acpx config: `permissionMode: "approve-all"`, `queueOwnerTtlSeconds: 300`

## What Happens

On every gateway startup, the embedded acpx runtime executes a probe for the `codex` agent regardless of `acp.allowedAgents`:

```
command: npx @zed-industries/codex-acp@^0.11.1
cwd: /home/moltbot/clawd
```

When the codex binary is missing (exit code non-zero or connection closed), the gateway logs:
```
[plugins] embedded acpx runtime backend probe failed: embedded ACP runtime probe failed (agent=codex; command=npx @zed-industries/codex-acp@^0.11.1; cwd=/home/moltbot/clawd; ACP connection closed)
```

If the codex subprocess crashes mid-stream before the probe completes, the gateway's write to its stdin causes:
```
[openclaw] Uncaught exception: Error: write EPIPE
```

This crashes the entire `openclaw-gateway.service` process, consuming **2min 28s of CPU** in the crash burst before systemd auto-restarts it ~6 seconds later.

## Expected vs Actual

| | Expected | Actual |
|---|---|---|
| Gateway startup with `acp.allowedAgents: ["claude","pi","opencode","openclaw"]` | ACPX runtime only initializes backends for listed agents | ACPX unconditionally probes `codex` |
| When codex binary is missing | Probe fails gracefully, logged as warning, gateway continues | Probe crashes gateway with EPIPE |

## ACP Identity Reconcile Also Fails

At startup, all 15 agent identity reconcile operations also fail:
```
[gateway] acp startup identity reconcile (renderer=v1): checked=15 resolved=0 failed=15
```

This suggests the codex probe failure may cascade to affect other ACP initialization steps.

## Reproduction Steps

1. Install OpenClaw with `acp.backend: "acpx"` and `acp.allowedAgents: ["claude","pi","opencode","openclaw"]`
2. Ensure `@zed-industries/codex-acp` is not installed, or the `codex` binary is broken/missing
3. Start/reload the gateway
4. Observe: crash with `write EPIPE` OR probe failure log for `agent=codex`

## Proposed Fix

Add a `probeOnStartup: false` config option to `plugins.entries.acpx.config` (or scope it to `acp.allowedAgents` filtering in the acpx runtime service initialization), so operators who use acpx for other agents (claude, pi, opencode, openclaw) without codex can disable this unconditional probe.

Alternatively, the acpx runtime should handle `write EPIPE` gracefully and treat it as a probe failure rather than an uncaught exception that crashes the gateway.

## Workaround

None available without removing the `acpx` plugin entirely (which breaks ACP dispatch for allowed agents).

## Bug Description

Config hot-reload mechanism causes Feishu account credentials (`appId`, `appSecret`) to be lost from `channels.feishu.accounts`.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Configure multiple Feishu accounts with valid `appId` and `appSecret`
2. Trigger a config hot-reload (e.g., modifying `gateway.auth.rateLimit`)
3. After reload, `channels.feishu.accounts` is empty/missing credentials

## Expected vs Actual

- **Expected**: Feishu accounts preserved after config hot-reload
- **Actual**: All `appId` and `appSecret` values are removed, leaving only `tools.doc` setting

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: 2026.4.14
- macOS
- Feishu plugin with multiple accounts (main, hr, broadcaster, researcher, gongzuozhuli)

## Relevant Log

```
[reload] config change detected; evaluating reload (gateway.auth.rateLimit)
[reload] config change detected; evaluating reload (channels.feishu.accounts, channels.feishu.enabled, channels.feishu.tools, channels.openclaw-weixin)
Config overwrite: openclaw.json (sha256 ... -> ..., backup=openclaw.json.bak)
```

Config audit log shows the write was done by the Gateway itself (argv: `gateway --port 18789`), not an external user command.

## Workaround

Restored from git backup containing the full Feishu account configuration.

## Severity

High — this causes complete loss of Feishu channel connectivity for all accounts.

items.title: Discord startup can fail hard on transient application-id lookup responses
items.body:
I hit what looks like a real startup bug in the Discord channel path while debugging a bot that briefly went offline during a reconnect wave.

The failure surfaced as:

`Failed to resolve Discord application id`

What seems to be happening:

- During Discord startup, OpenClaw fetches `oauth2/applications/@me` to resolve the application id.
- If that request throws, the code falls back to parsing the bot id from the token, which is good.
- But if Discord returns a non-OK HTTP response instead of throwing (for example a transient `429` during reconnect/rate-limit turbulence), startup returns `undefined` and the provider exits hard.
- In practice that means a valid bot token can still fail startup during a noisy reconnect window.

Relevant source path:

- `extensions/discord/src/probe.ts`
- `fetchDiscordApplicationId()`

Current behavior in that function is effectively:

- `res.ok` -> use `json.id`
- thrown transport error -> fall back to `parseApplicationIdFromToken(token)`
- non-OK HTTP response -> return `undefined`

Why this looks buggy:

The token already contains the bot/application id in its first segment, and OpenClaw is already willing to use that fallback for transport failures. That makes transient non-OK responses a strange hard-fail boundary, especially when the bot can often connect successfully on the very next attempt.

What I observed locally:

- one Discord account went offline
- logs showed `Failed to resolve Discord application id`
- later startup attempts for the same bot succeeded without changing the token or config
- the broader Discord environment at the time was unstable enough to also show websocket closes / reconnect churn

Expected behavior:

- keep failing fast for real auth problems like `401` / `403`
- but fall back to `parseApplicationIdFromToken(token)` for transient non-OK responses such as `429` or other temporary upstream failures

That would keep startup resilient without masking invalid credentials.

A simple version of the behavior change would be:

- `401` / `403` -> return `undefined`
- other non-OK responses -> fall back to token-derived application id
- thrown transport errors -> same fallback as today

I also added a local repro check around this path with a simulated `429`, and that matched the failure mode I was seeing.

Happy to open a PR if this direction makes sense.

items.title: Feature: built-in memory should support automatic context extraction and daily-log writing
items.body:
## Summary

The built-in memory experience should move beyond "remind the model to save important things" and support deterministic context extraction plus automatic daily-log writing.

Right now, `memory-core` is capable for indexing, search, promotion, and dreaming, but the actual capture path still depends too much on whether the model follows the prompt and decides to write memory files. That makes memory quality inconsistent.

## Problem

Current built-in memory behavior is still largely:
- auto index/search works
- memory flush can remind the model to save
- dreaming can later promote durable items
- but raw context capture into daily logs is not deterministic

In practice, this means the system can still miss important context when the model does not explicitly write `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` or `MEMORY.md` at the right time.

For users who want stronger memory guarantees, this is not enough.

## Requested behavior

The active memory plugin, especially `memory-core`, should support an optional built-in extraction pipeline that:

1. automatically extracts context from session transcripts or turn history
2. writes structured or semi-structured entries into daily logs (`memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) without relying purely on model obedience
3. separates short-term logging from long-term promotion
4. allows later promotion into `MEMORY.md` through existing dreaming/promotion logic

## Why this matters

There is a big difference between:
- "the model was reminded to save something"
- and
- "the platform deterministically extracted context and wrote a log entry"

The second one is much closer to what users expect from a real memory system.

## Suggested direction

A good direction would be to bring some ideas from `memory-lancedb-pro` into the built-in memory path, for example:

- automatic capture of relevant conversation context
- configurable extraction thresholds
- reflection / extraction pass before promotion
- separation between:
  - transcript capture
  - daily log generation
  - durable memory promotion

This does not need to fully duplicate third-party memory plugins, but the built-in default should be able to do more than prompt-based reminders.

## Proposed model

Something like this would make sense:

- **Stage 1: automatic capture**
  - deterministic extraction from transcripts / turns
  - write to daily note or structured short-term store

- **Stage 2: daily memory layer**
  - keep a reliable running log of important context

- **Stage 3: long-term promotion**
  - dreaming / promotion logic decides what graduates into `MEMORY.md`

## Related issues

- #13587 Feature Request: Native Cross-Session Prompt Logging
- #51572 Feature: fire session-memory hook on session reset/prune, not just compaction
- #67139 TKT-20260412-006: Memory Processing Engine

## Request

Please consider making automatic context extraction + daily-log writing a first-class built-in memory capability, instead of leaving built-in memory primarily dependent on whether the model follows save-to-memory instructions.

items.title: [Feature] Enable Playwright navigation interception for local-managed browser mode
items.body:
## Summary

Local-managed browser mode should use Playwright for navigation interception, just like remote-cdp mode does. This would enable redirect-hop inspection for SSRF policy enforcement, making the browser tool more secure and functional.

## Background

Currently, browser profile modes have different capabilities for navigation interception:

| Mode | `usesPersistentPlaywright` | Redirect Inspection |
|------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| Chrome MCP (`existing-session`) | `false` | ❌ None |
| Remote CDP | `true` | ✅ Playwright route handler |
| Local-managed | `false` | ❌ None |

The decision is based solely on `cdpIsLoopback`:

```typescript
// extensions/browser/src/browser/profile-capabilities.ts
if (!profile.cdpIsLoopback) {
  return { usesPersistentPlaywright: true };  // remote-cdp
}
return { usesPersistentPlaywright: false };   // local-managed
```

## Problem

### 1. Local-managed has higher SSRF risk but weaker protection

SSRF attacks are **more dangerous** in local-managed mode:
- OpenClaw and Chrome run on the same machine
- That machine typically has access to internal networks (databases, admin panels, internal APIs)
- SSRF attacks can directly access these internal resources

Remote-cdp mode actually has **lower** SSRF risk:
- Chrome runs on a remote machine
- SSRF attacks affect the remote machine's network, not OpenClaw's internal network

Yet the current design gives Playwright protection to remote-cdp (lower risk) but not to local-managed (higher risk).

### 2. `playwright-core` is already a dependency

OpenClaw already includes `playwright-core` as a dependency:

```json
// package.json
"playwright-core": "1.59.1"
```

`playwright-core` can connect to local Chrome via CDP:

```typescript
await chromium.connectOverCDP("ws://localhost:9222/...");
```

There's no technical barrier or additional dependency required.

### 3. Current workaround is broken

When `dangerouslyAllowPrivateNetwork: false` is configured:
- Hostname URLs are blocked (expected, needs redirect inspection)
- IP literal URLs are also blocked due to a bug in `requiresInspectableBrowserNavigationRedirectsForUrl` (#66109, #66268)

This makes local-managed mode completely unusable with strict SSRF policy.

### 4. Related issues

- #66109 - Browser SSRF Policy blocks all actions on hostname URLs
- #66268 - SSRF policy blocks hostname-based navigation even with profile=user
- #45153 - Sandbox browser ignores top-level browser.ssrfPolicy
- #57055 - Sandbox browser ignores global browser.ssrfPolicy configuration

## Proposed Solution

Enable Playwright navigation for local-managed mode:

```typescript
// Option 1: Always use Playwright for navigation
if (profile.driver === "existing-session") {
  return { usesPersistentPlaywright: false };  // Chrome MCP uses its own mechanism
}
return { usesPersistentPlaywright: true };     // Both local-managed and remote-cdp

// Option 2: Make it configurable
return { 
  usesPersistentPlaywright: profile.usePlaywright ?? !profile.cdpIsLoopback 
};
```

This would allow `gotoPageWithNavigationGuard` to intercept all navigation requests and check each redirect against SSRF policy.

## Benefits

1. **Better security**: Local-managed mode gets the same redirect-hop inspection as remote-cdp
2. **Consistent behavior**: All modes handle SSRF policy the same way
3. **Fixes usability**: IP literal URLs would work with strict SSRF policy (after fixing the separate bug in `requiresInspectableBrowserNavigationRedirectsForUrl`)
4. **No new dependencies**: `playwright-core` is already included

## Considerations

- **Performance**: Playwright connection has minimal overhead (cached after first use)
- **Backward compatibility**: Should not affect existing behavior for users without strict SSRF policy
- **Chrome MCP**: This proposal does not change Chrome MCP (`existing-session`) behavior

## Related Code

- `extensions/browser/src/browser/profile-capabilities.ts` - Mode detection
- `extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.tab-ops.ts` - Navigation logic
- `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts` - Playwright route handler (`gotoPageWithNavigationGuard`)

FAIL  src/agents/skills.test.ts > buildWorkspaceSkillCommandSpecs > includes enabled Claude bundle markdown commands as native OpenClaw slash commands

The test builds a compound-bundle plugin fixture with a `workflows-review.md` markdown command and expects `buildWorkspaceSkillCommandSpecs` to surface it as a native slash command. The returned commands array is empty.

items.title: Bug: User messages not displayed immediately in Control UI (optimistic update lost after history reload)
items.body:
### Bug: User messages not displayed immediately in Control UI

**Description:**  
When a user sends a message in the Control UI (webchat), the message is optimistically added to the local chatMessages state. However, the subsequent WebSocket event triggers `loadChatHistory()` which replaces the entire local `chatMessages` with the server response. Since the context has been compacted, the user's message appears as a metadata block instead of the original text.

**Steps to reproduce:**  
1. Open Control UI webchat  
2. Type and send a message  
3. Message does not appear immediately in the chat window  
4. After AI responds, the user's message appears as compressed metadata  

**Expected behavior:**  
User's message should appear immediately in the chat window (optimistic update) without waiting for the AI response.

**File:** `ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts`  
**Function:** `loadChatHistory()` and message sending flow

**Suggested fix:**  
When reloading chat history after sending a message, preserve locally pending/unconfirmed user messages rather than replacing them entirely with the server response. One approach would be to track locally sent messages (by their client-generated ID or content hash) and merge them with the server history instead of replacing.

items.title: CLI backend incremental streaming for channel preview (not final-only buffering)
items.body:
### Summary

Add an optional incremental streaming mode for CLI backends so channels can display progressive reply updates during long runs.

### Problem to solve

When using CLI backends (for example Codex CLI wrappers), the user often sees only typing/status for a long time and then one final message.
Even if the backend emits progressive stdout/events, channel UX still appears final-only in practice.
This is insufficient for long-running tasks because users cannot tell whether the run is healthy, stalled, or doing useful work.

### Proposed solution

 Introduce a configurable incremental streaming path for agents.defaults.cliBackends.*:

Add a backend-level streaming mode, e.g. streamingMode: "buffered" | "incremental" (default buffered for compatibility).
In incremental mode, forward eligible partial text/events from CLI output to channel preview updates (Telegram/Feishu/etc.) with debounce/coalescing controls.
Expose capability metadata (for routing/UX decisions), e.g. supports_cli_incremental_stream: true/false.
Add diagnostics to distinguish:
no incremental output produced by backend, vs
incremental output produced but buffered/dropped before channel dispatch.
Example config:

{
  "agents": {
    "defaults": {
      "cliBackends": {
        "codex-cli": {
          "streamingMode": "incremental"
        }
      }
    }
  }
}


### Alternatives considered

1. Stay with final-only buffered output: stable but poor UX on long tasks.
2. Inject placeholder/progress text manually: noisy, inconsistent, and can pollute conversation content.
3. Move all traffic to provider-backed SSE only: not always possible for CLI/account-switch workflows.

### Impact

1. Affected users/systems/channels: operators using CLI backends across Telegram/Feishu and other IM channels.
2. Severity: medium to high (can feel like blocked workflow on long tasks).
3. Frequency: frequent/intermittent depending on task length; common for long Codex runs.
4. Consequence: delayed user feedback, repeated retries, uncertainty about run state, extra manual monitoring.

### Evidence/examples

1. In our setup, backend (codex-switch with codex_server) can emit mid-run events, but chat surfaces still often show final-only behavior.
2. OpenClaw docs already note CLI backend limitation as “No streaming (CLI output is collected then returned)”, which matches observed UX.
3. We can provide reproducible logs/session traces if needed.

### Additional information

1. Backward compatibility is important: keep current behavior as default (buffered) and gate new behavior behind explicit config.
2. We do not require mid-stream failover; incremental preview alone would already solve the major UX pain.

items.title: [Security]: Log redaction parity across sinks — file logs, console, and diagnostic traces
items.body:
Sub-issue of #64046

## Maintainer Requirements

| Field | Content |
|---|---|
| **Scope** | File logs, console logs, diagnostic traces, and all direct log adapters |
| **Deliverable** | A shared redaction entry point consumed by every log sink |
| **Acceptance Criteria** | Sensitive fields/tokens are redacted in all sinks under default config; known leak scenarios from related issues have test coverage |

## Problem Statement

Console log output applies secret redaction, but **file logs, diagnostic traces, and other direct log adapters do not go through the same redaction gate**. This creates an inconsistency where secrets that are masked on screen are written in plaintext to persistent storage.

## Sink Status

| Priority | Sink | Default on? | Sensitivity | Gap |
|----------|------|:-----------:|:-----------:|-----|
| **P0** | Main file log | Yes | Critical | Raw `JSON.stringify(logObj)`, no redaction |
| **P0** | External transports | Yes | Critical | Plugins receive raw logObj |
| **P0** | Console capture relay | Yes | High | Forwarded to file logger unredacted |
| **P0** | Subsystem file path | Yes | High | Only console path has `consoleMessage` protection |
| **P1** | ACP spawn stream | Yes | Medium-High | JSONL fire-and-forget, `assistant_delta` may contain tokens |
| **P1** | Cache trace | Yes | Medium | `prompt`/`note`/`error` unredacted; other fields already sanitized |
| **P1** | Anthropic payload log | Yes | Medium | `error`/`usage` events written raw |
| **P2** | OTEL log export | No | Medium | Has standalone redaction, not shared gate |
| **P2** | Raw stream | No | High | Completely raw, off by default |
| **P2** | Config audit / cron log | Yes | Low | Low frequency |

## Core design

- **Shared gate** — new `redact-sink.ts`, all sinks call it at their write boundary
- **Sink-level, not call-site** — gate runs before `JSON.stringify` / `appendFile` / transport callback
- **Failure-safe** — redaction errors fall back to original content, no exceptions on hot path
- **Config-aware** — honors `logging.redactSensitive`; short-circuits when `"off"`
- **Defense in depth** — `log-tail` read-side redaction retained for old log files

## Breakdown

| PR | Scope | Summary |
|----|-------|---------|
| **Phase 1** | `src/logging/` | Shared gate + P0 sinks (file log, transports, console relay, subsystem file path) |
| **Phase 2** | `src/agents/` | P1 diagnostic traces (acp-stream, cache-trace, anthropic-payload-log) |
| **Phase 3+** | Extensions + long-tail | OTEL unification, raw-stream, config audit, extension sinks — based on maintainer feedback |

items.title: Gateway crashes on ENETUNREACH — uncaught network exception kills entire process
items.body:
## Summary

The gateway process exits with code 1 when a single outbound connection hits `ENETUNREACH`. A momentary network unreachability (e.g. Telegram's IP briefly unavailable) causes the entire gateway to crash, disconnecting all agents and sessions.

## Error from log

```
2026-04-16T18:44:37.946+07:00 [openclaw] Uncaught exception: Error: connect ENETUNREACH 149.154.167.220:443 - Local (0.0.0.0:62855)
    at internalConnect (node:net:1110:16)
    ...
    at connect (/Users/greg/codebase/openclaw/node_modules/undici/lib/core/connect.js:70:20)
ELIFECYCLE  Command failed with exit code 1.
```

`149.154.167.220` is a Telegram API IP. The connection error bubbled up as an uncaught exception and terminated the Node process.

## Impact

- All TUI clients show "gateway disconnected: closed | idle"
- All agent sessions drop — "not connected to gateway — message not sent"
- Gateway must be manually restarted
- Any in-progress agent work is lost

## Expected behavior

A failed outbound network connection (even a persistent one) should be caught and handled gracefully — log the error, back off and retry, and keep the gateway running. A single provider's network being temporarily unreachable should never take down the whole process.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Run gateway with Telegram connected
2. Briefly interrupt network connectivity to Telegram's IPs (or have ISP/routing hiccup)
3. Gateway exits immediately

## Suggested fix

- Wrap the Telegram connection/polling loop in a try/catch or attach an `error` event handler on the underlying socket/agent so `ENETUNREACH` is caught at the connection level
- Add a top-level `process.on('uncaughtException')` handler that logs the error and decides whether it is fatal rather than always crashing
- Alternatively, wrap outbound requests in a circuit breaker that absorbs transient network errors

items.title: Normal sessions can inherit stale subagent lineage and incorrectly hit sessions_spawn depth limits
items.body:
## Summary

Normal top-level sessions can inherit stale subagent lineage metadata, which causes later ordinary runs to be treated as nested child sessions. That can make `sessions_spawn` fail with depth-limit errors like:

- `sessions_spawn is not allowed at this depth (current depth: 2, max: 1)`

...even when the visible conversation is a normal top-level group/direct chat.

## Root cause

Session metadata for ordinary turns currently preserves subagent lineage fields from prior state:

- `spawnedBy`
- `spawnDepth`
- `subagentRole`
- `subagentControlScope`
- related parent lineage fields

Later normal runs can then reuse the stored lineage instead of only using live spawned-session context.

Depth checks in `sessions_spawn` ultimately derive nesting from this persisted lineage, so stale metadata can make an ordinary chat turn look like a nested session.

## Impact

- normal chats can lose the ability to spawn subagents
- behavior is confusing because the visible session looks top-level
- failures are sticky because the stale lineage keeps being persisted across future turns

## Proposed fix

- clear subagent lineage fields on ordinary top-level session turns
- preserve lineage only for actual spawned/subagent sessions
- avoid reusing stale `sessionEntry.spawnedBy` as normal-run spawn context
- add regression coverage for a top-level session after stale lineage was previously present

## Notes

This appears to be a product bug in session-state persistence/reuse, not a configuration problem.

items.title: REM topic extraction produces role-tag / boilerplate "themes" on chat transcripts (frequency-based signal is structurally unsuited to chat corpus)
items.body:
## Summary

On a real personal-assistant chat deployment (heavy Telegram/BlueBubbles/WhatsApp traffic + cron agent turns), REM topic extraction consistently promotes **transcript structural words** (`user`, `assistant`, role tags, boilerplate replies like "好的" / "HEARTBEAT_OK") as top themes, with `confidence=1.00` and `recalls=0`, even after input-layer sanitization is in place.

## Evidence

Local short-term-recall snapshot contains staged candidates like:

```
Theme: `assistant` kept surfacing across 1559 memories.  confidence: 1.00
Theme: `user` kept surfacing across ...  confidence: 1.00
status: staged  recalls: 0
```

This is after all current input cleaning landed:
- #66548 (strip inbound envelopes from user turns) — mine, merged
- #67315 (skip dreaming narrative transcripts before bootstrap records land)
- Local patch to skip cron session transcripts during ingestion

So the candidates above are what REM produces on **clean** input.

## Why stopwords won't fix it

Filtering `user`/`assistant`/role-tag tokens would only hand the crown to the next high-frequency class: polite acknowledgments (`好的`, `明白了`, "ok"), system-prompt structural words, or in gamified cron channels, game-state tokens (`繁荣`, `Lv2`). A chat transcript's token-frequency distribution is dominated by conversational scaffolding, not by topical content.

## Root cause (hypothesis)

REM's current signal — pick high-frequency tokens/phrases across session corpus as "themes that kept surfacing" — is a **structural mismatch** with conversational data. It works on document corpora (TF-IDF assumptions hold) but not on chat, where the same 10–30 scaffold words dominate every session regardless of topic.

## Suggested directions (not exhaustive)

1. **Replace frequency signal with LLM daily reflection**: once/day, hand the transcript to an LLM and ask for 3–5 concrete insight bullets. This is effectively what ad-hoc agent memory writes already do successfully (daily briefing cron, explicit \`memory_add\` calls).
2. **Embedding-based outlier/cluster detection**: pick representative or novel segments, let an LLM label them. Aligns with Letta/MemGPT/Zep/mem0.
3. **Entity-gated extraction**: only count tokens that match named-entity / numeric / proper-noun filters before frequency scoring. Cheap but unlikely to reach 3-5's precision.

Happy to share more from the local snapshot if useful.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.4.15
- Mixed channels: Telegram (private + groups), BlueBubbles, WhatsApp, cron agents
- 5 agent workspaces, large session history

items.title: [Bug]: channels status --probe always times out with 8+ accounts due to sequential probing
items.body:
## Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

## Beta release blocker

No

## Summary

`openclaw channels status --probe` always times out at the default 10s limit when multiple channel accounts are configured, because the gateway probes accounts sequentially and the total probe time exceeds the CLI timeout.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Configure 8 channel accounts (1 Discord + 6 Telegram + 1 Feishu) in `openclaw.json`
2. Run `openclaw channels status --probe`
3. Observe timeout after 10 seconds with "Gateway not reachable" error

Workaround: `openclaw channels status --probe --timeout 30000` succeeds but takes ~61 seconds.

## Expected behavior

`channels status --probe` should return results within the default timeout, or at minimum report partial results from accounts that finished probing before the deadline.

## Actual behavior

```
$ openclaw channels status --probe
Checking channel status (probe)…
Gateway not reachable: Error: gateway timeout after 10000ms
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
```

The CLI times out and falls back to config-only status (no probe data). All probe work completed on the gateway side is discarded.

With `--timeout 30000`:
```
$ openclaw channels status --probe --timeout 30000
# Succeeds after ~61s total, showing all 8 accounts probed successfully
```

## Root cause analysis

Two problems compound:

### 1. Sequential account probing in gateway

`src/gateway/server-methods/channels.ts:136-164` — accounts are probed in a nested sequential loop:

```typescript
for (const plugin of plugins) {        // sequential across channels
  for (const accountId of accountIds) { // sequential across accounts
    probeResult = await plugin.status.probeAccount({
      account,
      timeoutMs,  // each call up to 10s
    });
  }
}
```

With 8 accounts and Telegram probes using 3-retry exponential backoff plus proxy overhead, total time reaches 54-61s.

### 2. Timeout budget mismatch

- **CLI timeout** (`src/commands/channels/status.ts:283`): default 10s, shared by WS connect + auth + full probe cycle
- **Per-account probe timeout** (`channels.ts:84-85`): also 10s
- **Maximum sequential time**: 8 accounts x 10s = 80s possible

The CLI timeout (10s) is less than the minimum expected probe time for multi-account deployments.

## OpenClaw version

2026.4.15

## Operating system

macOS 26.2 (Darwin 25.2.0)

## Install method

npm global

## Model

zai/glm-5.1

## Provider / routing chain

openclaw -> zai

## Logs, screenshots, and evidence

Successful run with extended timeout showing real probe duration:
```
$ time openclaw channels status --probe --timeout 30000
# ... all 8 accounts probed ok ...
openclaw channels status --probe --timeout 30000  65.20s user 6.55s system 116% cpu 1:01.56 total
```

`openclaw health` completes in ~600ms because it uses a different code path (single aggregated health check vs per-account probe).

## Impact and severity

- **Affected**: Any deployment with 5+ channel accounts
- **Severity**: Medium-High -- `--probe` is the primary tool for verifying channel connectivity after config changes, and it silently returns stale config-only data
- **Frequency**: Always reproducible with enough accounts
- **Consequence**: Operators cannot verify channel health without knowing the `--timeout` workaround; misleading "Gateway not reachable" error message suggests a gateway problem when the issue is probe latency

## Suggested fix

**Parallel probing + adjusted timeout budget:**

1. In `channels.ts`, replace the sequential `for...await` loop with `Promise.allSettled()` to probe all accounts concurrently
2. Increase the CLI default timeout for `--probe` from 10s to 30s (accounts for one slow/failing probe without penalizing all others)

Expected improvement: 8 accounts probed in ~8s (time of slowest single probe) instead of ~60s (sum of all probes), well within a 30s default timeout.

This is a change to `src/gateway/server-methods/channels.ts` only — the per-account `probeAccount` contract stays the same.

## Additional information

NOT_ENOUGH_INFO
