items.title: Control UI: allow customizing chat message max-width via config
items.body:
## Problem

The Control UI hard-codes `.chat-group-messages { max-width: min(900px, 68%) }` in the bundled CSS. On wide monitors (≥1440px), this leaves massive blank space on the right side of the chat area, making the UI feel cramped and wasteful.

Every OpenClaw upgrade overwrites the CSS bundle, so any manual patch is lost. Users currently have to re-patch minified CSS after every update.

## Proposed Solution

Add a config option (e.g. `webchat.chatMaxWidth` or `controlUI.chatMaxWidth`) that injects a CSS custom property like `--chat-max-width` into the UI shell. The CSS rule would then read:

```css
.chat-group-messages {
  max-width: var(--chat-max-width, min(900px, 68%));
}
```

This lets users customize via `openclaw.json` without touching the bundle:

```json
{
  "webchat": {
    "chatMaxWidth": "min(1280px, 82%)"
  }
}
```

## Alternatives Considered

- **Custom CSS file injection**: A `controlUI.customCssPath` option that loads an additional stylesheet. More flexible but heavier.
- **Manual patching**: Current workaround. Fragile, breaks on every upgrade.

## Environment

- OpenClaw 2026.4.14
- macOS arm64
- Wide monitor (2560×1440+)

items.title: [feature request] Auto-apply auth.applyConfig at plugin-load time for Category B providers (defineSingleProviderPluginEntry)
items.body:
## Context

Provider plugins split into two registration categories today:

- **Category A** — `definePluginEntry` + an explicit `register.runtime.ts` that calls `api.registerProvider` with a fully-built `ProviderPlugin` including its model catalog (anthropic, openai, google, xai, mistral, openrouter, amazon-bedrock). Catalog is visible to the runtime model registry at plugin-load time.
- **Category B** — `defineSingleProviderPluginEntry` with `catalog.buildProvider`. The provider shell registers at plugin-load, but the model catalog only lands in `config.models.providers.<id>` after an interactive `openclaw models auth login` flow calls `auth[i].applyConfig(cfg)`. The moonshot, deepseek, qwen, fireworks, together, and kilocode plugins are in this class.

For users who run OpenClaw interactively on a laptop, this gap is invisible — `openclaw models auth login` is part of the first-run path and the config gets populated naturally. For users who run OpenClaw headlessly (CI, Docker, a managed runtime, a pre-provisioned VM) and supply the provider API key via environment variable only, the gap becomes user-visible:

```
[gateway] startup model warmup failed for moonshot/kimi-k2.5: Error: Unknown model: moonshot/kimi-k2.5
```

even when `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` is correctly set in the process environment and the `moonshot` plugin is enabled. The runtime refuses the model because `config.models.providers.moonshot` wasn't materialized.

Today, downstream headless wrappers work around this by hand-mirroring the `models.providers.<id>` block in their generated `openclaw.json`. I maintain [openclaw-managed-agents](https://github.com/stainlu/openclaw-managed-agents) which currently does exactly this for moonshot in [docker/entrypoint.sh](https://github.com/stainlu/openclaw-managed-agents/blob/main/docker/entrypoint.sh) (search for `PROVIDER_BLOCK_JSON`). Every extension to a new Category B provider duplicates the logic; the hand-mirrored blocks drift from the plugin's actual catalog whenever prices or model IDs change.

I verified the gap empirically with a probe on 2026-04-17: temporarily removed the hand-mirror from my downstream and ran a two-turn e2e — it reproduced the "Unknown model" error within ~44 seconds of the first user message.

## Proposed behavior change

Let Category B plugins **opt in** to auto-applying their `auth[i].applyConfig` at plugin-load time when the auth method's declared `envVar` is resolvable from the process environment.

Shape I had in mind:

```ts
// src/plugin-sdk/provider-entry.ts — additive field on existing type
export type SingleProviderPluginApiKeyAuthOptions = /* existing fields */ & {
  /**
   * When true: if this auth method's envVar is set in the current process
   * environment AND applyConfig is defined, call applyConfig(cfg) as part
   * of the plugin's startup config-merge, so the resulting config has
   * models.providers.<id> populated with the plugin's catalog.
   *
   * Default: false — every existing plugin (first-party and third-party)
   * is unaffected unless it explicitly opts in.
   */
  autoApplyWhenEnvResolved?: boolean;
};
```

Opt-in per auth method so every existing plugin keeps current behavior. Downstream for the moonshot plugin this is a 1-line diff per auth entry; same pattern works for deepseek and qwen.

## What I can't answer from reading the tree

Before I write the PR I want to confirm the right seam for the mutation. Specifically:

1. **Does `OpenClawPluginApi` already expose a config-mutation hook I should use?** I looked at `src/plugin-sdk/plugin-entry.ts` and `src/plugins/types.ts` and didn't spot one, but there's a lot of surface and I may have missed an intended path (e.g. a "queue a config default mutator that runs during the next merge pass" helper).
2. **If not, where's the right place to add one?** My rough sketch is something like `api.queueDefaultConfigMutation(mutator: (cfg: OpenClawConfig) => OpenClawConfig)` that runs as part of the existing config-defaults pipeline, so user-supplied `openclaw.json` still wins when it disagrees with the plugin default. But I'd rather align with whatever pattern exists for Category A defaults than invent a new one.
3. **Are there ordering pitfalls** around plugin-load vs config-merge I should know about? I want user config to win on conflicts (e.g. a user-supplied alternate Moonshot `baseUrl`).

## Test shape (if the design holds)

In `src/plugin-sdk/provider-entry.test.ts`:

- `autoApplyWhenEnvResolved: true` + env var set + `applyConfig` present → mutation observable on the merged config
- `autoApplyWhenEnvResolved: true` + env var missing → no call to `applyConfig`
- `autoApplyWhenEnvResolved: false` (default) → no call regardless
- `autoApplyWhenEnvResolved: true` + `applyConfig` undefined → no throw, no call
- User `openclaw.json` takes precedence over the auto-applied default (ordering test)

## Version impact

Additive, backwards-compatible → minor version bump. No third-party plugin is affected until it opts in. Docs updates in `docs/plugins/sdk-provider-plugins.md` and `docs/plugins/sdk-entrypoints.md` to mention the new field.

## Related

- PR #67928 — real Moonshot prices in `extensions/moonshot/provider-catalog.ts`. Independent of this change and landing-ready on its own; combined with the auto-apply mechanism, the downstream `PROVIDER_BLOCK_JSON` hack goes away entirely.
- [Downstream context](https://github.com/stainlu/openclaw-managed-agents/blob/main/docs/architecture.md) — how openclaw-managed-agents currently works around this gap.

## Ask

Quick design check before I write the PR:

1. Is there already a config-mutation API on `OpenClawPluginApi` I should use, or do I add one?
2. If adding one, what shape fits the existing plugin-load / config-merge pipeline best?
3. Any concerns about the opt-in-per-auth-method shape above, or would you prefer the flag at the plugin level or on the catalog config?

Happy to iterate on the design here before committing code.

items.title: [Bug]: OpenClaw 2026.4.15 Image Upload Bug Report
items.body:
### Bug type

Regression (worked before, now fails)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

**Control UI image paste/upload functionality is broken in OpenClaw 2026.4.15, but works correctly in 2026.3.13**

## Environment
- **OS**: macOS 25.4.0 (arm64)
- **Node**: v24.14.0
- **Working Version**: 2026.3.13 (61d171a)
- **Broken Version**: 2026.4.15 (041266a)
- **Interface**: OpenClaw Control UI (`http://127.0.0.1:18789/<basePath>/`)

## Environment
- **OS**: macOS 25.4.0 (arm64)
- **Node**: v24.14.0
- **Working Version**: 2026.3.13 (61d171a)
- **Broken Version**: 2026.4.15 (041266a)
- **Interface**: OpenClaw Control UI (`http://127.0.0.1:18789/<basePath>/`)

## Technical Investigation
### Configuration Analysis
- Model config correctly includes `"input": ["text", "image"]` in both versions
- Gateway HTTP endpoints enabled for both versions
- No errors in gateway.log related to attachment processing
- WebSocket messages show normal chat operations but no attachment payloads

### Root Cause Hypothesis
The issue appears to be in the **Control UI frontend code** in 2026.4.15:
- Attachment handling logic may have been modified/broken
- WebSocket message format for attachments may have changed without backend compatibility
- Client-side paste event handlers may not be properly capturing image data

### Version Comparison
- **2026.3.13**: Control UI assets work correctly with image paste
- **2026.4.15**: Control UI assets (`/dist/control-ui/assets/`) contain broken attachment handling

## Impact
- Users cannot use multimodal capabilities through Control UI interface
- Screenshot analysis, image recognition, and visual content processing are unavailable
- Forces users to either downgrade or use alternative interfaces (standard webchat)

## Workaround
Downgrade to OpenClaw 2026.3.13:
```bash
npm install -g openclaw@2026.3.13
openclaw gateway restart
```

## Additional Notes
- Issue affects Control UI specifically; standard webchat interface may work differently
- Both NVM-managed and globally-installed OpenClaw instances affected
- Problem persists across different image formats (PNG, JPG, etc.)
- No console errors visible in browser dev tools during paste attempts

## Logs Evidence
Gateway logs show successful message processing but no attachment handling:
```
# Working version (2026.3.13) would show attachment processing
# Broken version (2026.4.15) shows only regular chat messages:
[ws] ⇄ res ✓ chat.history 1304ms conn=... id=...
```

## Recommendation
This appears to be a regression introduced in 2026.4.15 Control UI frontend code. Recommend:
1. Audit recent changes to Control UI attachment handling code
2. Verify WebSocket message format compatibility between frontend and backend
3. Add integration tests for image paste functionality
4. Consider hotfix release if confirmed as widespread issue

### Steps to reproduce

1. Install OpenClaw 2026.4.15 via npm: `npm install -g openclaw@2026.4.15`
2. Start Gateway service
3. Open Control UI in browser
4. Attempt to paste an image (Cmd+V) or drag-drop an image file
5. Observe that image is not processed/received by the agent
6. Check gateway.log - no attachment-related log entries appear
7. Downgrade to 2026.3.13: `npm install -g openclaw@2026.3.13`
8. Restart Gateway
9. Repeat step 4 - image paste now works correctly

### Expected behavior

Can understand image‘s content correctly.

### Actual behavior

Pasting images (Cmd+V) or drag-and-drop upload does not work

### OpenClaw version

2026.04.15

### Operating system

macOS 26.4

### Install method

_No response_

### Model

bailian/qwen3-max-2026-01-23

### Provider / routing chain

bailian

│ memory-core   │ openclaw │ loaded   │ stock:memory-core/index.js    │ 2026.4.15 │

items.title: [Bug] Dream Diary outputs to stdout instead of DREAMS.md
items.body:
## Bug Description

Dreaming feature is enabled and runs at 3 AM, but the Dream Diary (human-readable narrative output) is printed to stdout/terminal instead of being written to DREAMS.md file as documented.

## Environment

- OpenClaw version: 2026.4.12 (1c0672b)
- OS: Windows
- Memory backend: memory-core with dreaming enabled

## Expected Behavior

According to the official documentation:
- Dreaming writes human-readable narrative output to \DREAMS.md\ (or existing \dreams.md\)
- Dream Diary should be written after each phase with enough material

## Actual Behavior

1. ✅ Dreaming cron triggers at 3 AM (confirmed via cron logs)
2. ✅ Light phase completes - writes to \memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md\ with \## Light Sleep\ block
3. ✅ REM phase completes - writes to \memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md\ with \## REM Sleep\ block
4. ✅ Dream Diary (narrative text) is generated and appears in terminal stdout
5. ❌ **DREAMS.md file is NOT created**
6. ❌ Deep phase never runs (all entries have recallCount=0, not meeting minRecallCount≥3 threshold)

## Evidence

- Terminal output shows poetic Dream Diary narrative (e.g., reflections about memory consolidation)
- \memory/2026-04-17.md\ contains Light/REM phase output (internal working logs)
- \DREAMS.md\ does not exist in workspace
- \.dreams/events.jsonl\ shows \storageMode: inline\ which writes to daily memory files instead of DREAMS.md

## Questions

1. Is the Dream Diary supposed to write to DREAMS.md or stdout?
2. Is \storageMode: inline\ the cause of DREAMS.md not being created?
3. Why do all recall entries have \ecallCount: 0\ despite having recall timestamps?

## Related Issues

- Issue #61421: Docs mention Dreaming config/commands but 2026.4.2 local build does not expose them
- Issue #67397: Dreaming cron skipped by heartbeat activeHours

items.title: [Bug]: wiki bridge CLI does not load the active memory slot plugin, so bridge mode falsely reports 0 exported artifacts
items.body:
## Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

## Beta release blocker

No

## Summary

`openclaw wiki status` and `openclaw wiki bridge import` falsely reported `0 exported artifacts` in bridge mode even though the active memory plugin exported 67 public artifacts in the same environment when loaded in-process.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Configure OpenClaw with `plugins.slots.memory = "memory-core"`.
2. Enable `memory-wiki` in bridge mode with `memory-wiki.config.bridge.readMemoryArtifacts = true`.
3. Run `openclaw wiki status --json`.
4. Run `openclaw wiki bridge import --json`.
5. Observe `bridgePublicArtifactCount: 0` and `artifactCount: 0`.
6. In the same environment, load the plugins in-process and call `listActiveMemoryPublicArtifacts(...)`.
7. Observe that the in-process path returns 67 artifacts.

## Expected behavior

The `wiki` CLI path should load the active memory slot plugin and report the same bridge artifact availability observed in-process. In this case, the grounded expected result is non-zero bridge artifacts, because the same environment returned 67 artifacts once the configured memory slot plugin was loaded.

## Actual behavior

The `wiki` CLI path loaded only `memory-wiki`, did not load the configured memory slot plugin, and reported zero exported artifacts:

- `bridgePublicArtifactCount: 0`
- `artifactCount: 0`

Cross-checks from the same environment:

- full plugin load: `67` artifacts
- wiki-only load: `0`
- memory-core-only load: `67`

## OpenClaw version

2026.4.15

## Operating system

Ubuntu 24.04.4 LTS on WSL2

## Install method

npm global

## Model

NOT_ENOUGH_INFO

## Provider / routing chain

NOT_ENOUGH_INFO

## Additional provider/model setup details

NOT_ENOUGH_INFO

## Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```text
Before local patch:
- `openclaw wiki status --json` reported `bridgePublicArtifactCount: 0`
- `openclaw wiki bridge import --json` reported `artifactCount: 0`

Cross-check from the same environment:
- full plugin load: `67`
- wiki-only load: `0`
- memory-core-only load: `67`

Local fix that resolved the issue:
- augment the `wiki` command-scoped plugin load with the configured `plugins.slots.memory` plugin id

After local patch:
- `openclaw wiki status --json` reported `bridgePublicArtifactCount: 67`
- `pageCounts.source: 67`
- `warnings: []`
- `openclaw wiki bridge import --json` reported `artifactCount: 67`, `skippedCount: 67`, `workspaces: 1`
```

## Impact and severity

Affected: users running `memory-wiki` in bridge mode with a separate memory slot plugin such as `memory-core`
Severity: Medium, because the CLI reports false bridge failure and makes the feature look broken
Frequency: Reproduced consistently in the observed setup until locally patched
Consequence: users can be misled into thinking no public memory artifacts exist, and normal wiki bridge CLI usage appears broken

## Additional information

Root cause identified locally: the CLI command-scoped loader for `openclaw wiki ...` only loaded the plugin activated by the `wiki` command (`memory-wiki`). It did not also load the configured `plugins.slots.memory` plugin, so no memory capability/public artifacts were registered in that process.

A local patch to the CLI loader fixed the issue by adding the configured memory slot plugin to the `wiki` command-scoped plugin set. The fix was generic and did not hardcode `memory-core`.

items.title: [Bug]: Local assistant attachments shown as \"Unavailable — Outside allowed folders\" despite correct server config
items.body:
### Bug type

Behavior bug (incorrect output/state without crash)

### Beta release blocker

No

### Summary

Local assistant media attachments (e.g. TTS audio in `MEDIA:` lines) render as \"Unavailable — Outside allowed folders\" in Control UI even when the server's `localMediaPreviewRoots` includes the path and the server-side `assistant-media?meta=1` endpoint confirms `{\"available\": true}`.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Run OpenClaw with Control UI enabled and a config that produces local media (e.g. TTS generating `.mp3` files under `~/.openclaw/media/outbound/`). 
2. Trigger an assistant response that includes a `MEDIA:/home/node/.openclaw/media/outbound/<uuid>.mp3` line.\n
3. Observe the attachment card in the Control UI webchat

### Expected behavior

The attachment renders as playable audio with a working player and download link, since the path is inside the server-configured `localMediaPreviewRoots` and the server-side meta check returns `available: true`

### Actual behavior

The attachment renders as \"Unavailable — Outside allowed folders\" with a blocked status card. The server-side meta endpoint (`/__openclaw__/assistant-media?source=...&meta=1`) returns `{\"available\": true}` and the raw media endpoint returns `200 audio/mpeg`, confirming the server considers the path valid

### OpenClaw version

2026.4.15

### Operating system

Debian 12 (bookworm) on Linux 6.12.0 / OpenShift pod

### Install method

docker (OpenShift)

### Model

Any (not model-dependent — bug is in the UI rendering path)

### Provider / routing chain

UI bug

### Additional provider/model setup details

_No response_

### Logs, screenshots, and evidence

```shell
# Server-side meta endpoint confirms attachment is available:
GET /__openclaw__/assistant-media?source=/home/node/.openclaw/media/outbound/8ad46428-7c81-412e-9df8-e1dbab658b57.mp3&meta=1
→ 200 {\"available\":true}

# Raw media fetch works:
GET /__openclaw__/assistant-media?source=/home/node/.openclaw/media/outbound/8ad46428-7c81-412e-9df8-e1dbab658b57.mp3
→ 200 audio/mpeg (valid bytes)

# Bootstrap config includes the correct root:
GET /__openclaw__/control-ui-config.json
→ 200 { \"localMediaPreviewRoots\": [\"/home/node/.openclaw/media\", ...] }

# But the UI renders: \"Unavailable — Outside allowed folders\"\n\n\nRoot cause traced to `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts` in `resolveAssistantAttachmentAvailability()`:

// Line 747 — runs BEFORE bootstrap config has loaded (roots still [])
if (!isLocalAttachmentPreviewAllowed(source, localMediaPreviewRoots)) {
  return { status: \"unavailable\", reason: \"Outside allowed folders\", checkedAt: Date.now() };
}


When `localMediaPreviewRoots` is `[]` (bootstrap config fetch not yet complete), `isLocalAttachmentPreviewAllowed()` returns `false` for every local path. This hard-returns before reaching the server-side meta check, which is authoritative and would return `available: true`
```

### Impact and severity

- Affected: All Control UI webchat users viewing assistant responses that include local media attachments (TTS audio, generated images, documents)
- Severity: Medium (attachments appear broken despite being fully available server-side) 
- Frequency: Deterministic on page load / session start when attachments render before bootstrap config completes
- Consequence: Local assistant media is inaccessible in the UI until a manual page refresh (and even then, only if refresh timing allows bootstrap to complete before render)

### Additional information

The client-side `isLocalAttachmentPreviewAllowed` check is a UX optimisation to avoid unnecessary server round-trips for paths clearly outside allowed roots. However, it should not hard-deny when roots haven't loaded yet — the server-side `meta=1` endpoint should be the fallback authority.

A secondary factor is that the bootstrap config endpoint was served with only `Cache-Control: no-cache`. While not the primary cause, `no-store` would be more appropriate for a config endpoint whose values change between sessions."

### Additional information

_No response_

items.title: Bug: Image Cache - Different Images Result in Identical Cached Files (Same MD5)
items.body:
# Bug Report: Image Cache Bug - Same MD5 for Different Images

## Summary
When receiving images via Feishu/WeChat Work/WeChat bots, different images result in identical cached files (same MD5 hash), causing the AI to incorrectly describe images.

## Severity
High - Breaks image recognition functionality

## Environment
- OpenClaw version: Latest (2026.4.x)
- Platform: Windows 11
- Channels: Feishu (飞书), WeChat Work (企微), WeChat (微信)
- Model: MiniMax-M2.7

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Send an image via Feishu/WeChat Work/WeChat bot
2. Note the cached file location and MD5
3. Send a different image
4. Check the new cached file's MD5

## Expected vs Actual Behavior
**Expected:** Each different image should have a unique file and MD5 hash
**Actual:** All images result in the same file with identical MD5 hash

## Evidence
Multiple images sent at different times all have the same MD5:
```
$ ls -lt ~/.openclaw/media/inbound/
-rw-r--r-- 1 aiden 1186078 Apr 17 10:08 597df71d-b6e4-4b1e-82f1-700f7d515e5c.jpg
-rw-r--r-- 1 aiden 1186078 Apr 17 09:55 4f88147f-54b9-4abe-a6f4-6d3675e0d6b2.jpg
-rw-r--r-- 1 aiden 1186078 Apr 17 09:52 bb1cc2de-bd0b-4489-836a-cb8de59c1685.jpg
-rw-r--r-- 1 aiden 1186078 Apr 17 09:10 fea9db32-60a2-447a-bb29-99921cdbfb08.jpg

$ md5sum ~/.openclaw/media/inbound/*.jpg
daf8ac4f454d70d44ec4c570b0511449 .../597df71d-b6e4-4b1e-82f1-700f7d515e5c.jpg
daf8ac4f454d70d44ec4c570b0511449 .../4f88147f-54b9-4abe-a6f4-6d3675e0d6b2.jpg
daf8ac4f454d70d44ec4c570b0511449 .../bb1cc2de-bd0b-4489-836a-cb8de59c1685.jpg
daf8ac4f454d70d44ec4c570b0511449 .../fea9db32-60a2-447a-bb29-99921cdbfb08.jpg
```

All 4 different images have **identical MD5**: `daf8ac4f454d70d44ec4c570b0511449`

## Impact
- AI receives the same cached image regardless of what image is sent
- Image recognition returns incorrect descriptions based on wrong image content
- Example: User sends TARS robot photo, AI describes "domestic LLM comparison screenshot"

## Additional Context
- Text messages work correctly
- The issue persists across OpenClaw restarts
- Cache clearing does not fix the issue
- Affected all three channels (飞书、企微、微信)

items.title: [Bug]: Dreaming overwrites daily memory files (memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md) instead of only writing to memory/.dreams/
items.body:
## Describe the bug

The Dreaming (memory consolidation) feature incorrectly overwrites the daily conversation log files (`memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) with Dreaming output, instead of writing only to its designated directories (`memory/.dreams/` and `DREAMS.md`).

This results in the loss of daily conversation logs — the original dialogue content is replaced by Dreaming summaries.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Enable Dreaming in `openclaw.json`:
   ```json
   "plugins": {
     "entries": {
       "memory-core": {
         "config": {
           "dreaming": {
             "enabled": true
           }
         }
       }
     }
   }
   ```
2. Use OpenClaw normally for conversations over at least one day
3. Wait for Dreaming to trigger (runs during the night, ~3 AM)
4. Check `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` for the previous day(s)

## Expected behavior

Dreaming should write its output **only** to:
- `memory/.dreams/` (session corpus, dream content)
- `DREAMS.md` (dream diary summaries)

The daily conversation log files (`memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) should remain untouched — they are raw conversation records, not Dreaming output targets.

## Actual behavior

`memory/2026-04-15.md` and `memory/2026-04-16.md` were **completely overwritten** with Dreaming-generated content (dream narratives/summaries). The original conversation logs were lost from these files.

The original conversation content was still recoverable in `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/2026-04-15.txt`, but the primary daily log files were corrupted.

## Environment

- **OS:** Linux 6.6.87.2-microsoft-standard-WSL2 (x64)
- **Node:** v22.22.2
- **OpenClaw version:** 2026.4.5 (CLI) / 2026.4.14 (Gateway)
- **Memory plugin:** memory-core
- **Dreaming:** enabled

## Additional context

- The issue was first noticed on 2026-04-16
- Two consecutive daily files were affected: `memory/2026-04-15.md` and `memory/2026-04-16.md`
- Dreaming appears to be writing its consolidation output to the wrong file path
- This is a data integrity issue — users expect `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` to contain conversation logs, not Dreaming summaries
- As a workaround, Dreaming has been disabled (`"enabled": false`)

## Suggested fix

Dreaming should have explicit guards to ensure it never writes to files matching the `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md` pattern. Its output should be restricted to the `memory/.dreams/` directory and `DREAMS.md`.

items.title: [FEATURE] Session End Hook for Automatic Commitment/Memory Capture
items.body:
### Summary

Add a session end hook to automatically capture and save important information (commitments, plans, promises) from conversation history to memory files, preventing trust issues when AI "forgets" what was said.

### Problem to solve

**Current Behavior**:
When a conversation ends, important information (commitments, plans, promises) is only stored in session history but NOT automatically saved to memory files. This information is lost unless the AI manually writes it to memory.

**Real-World Example**:
1. User says: "I promise to start the evaluation framework tomorrow (4/18)"
2. AI acknowledges but doesn't save to memory
3. Next day, user asks: "Did you start the evaluation framework as promised?"
4. AI searches memory files → nothing found
5. AI searches session history → finds the promise, but too late
6. Trust is broken

**Current Workaround**:
- Daily reflection cron job manually checks session history
- Relies on AI's judgment to identify important content
- Unreliable and error-prone

### Proposed solution

Add a **Session End Hook** that triggers automatically when a session ends or at scheduled intervals.

**Hook Configuration** (in openclaw.json):
```json
{
  "session": {
    "endHook": {
      "enabled": true,
      "script": "path/to/capture-script.ps1",
      "keywords": ["承诺", "我会", "明天", "计划", "评估框架"],
      "targetFile": "D:\\Documents\\self-improvement\\commitments-tracker.md"
    }
  }
}
```

**Hook Execution**:
- Trigger: Session end OR scheduled interval (e.g., every 6 hours)
- Input: Session history (last N messages)
- Output: Extracted content to memory file

**Keyword-Based Extraction**:
- Scan session messages for commitment-related keywords
- Extract context (what, when, who)
- Append to tracking file

### Alternatives considered

1. **Manual Memory Writing**: Current approach, unreliable - depends on AI remembering to save
2. **Daily Cron Job**: Works but delayed (not real-time), only checks once per day
3. **User Reminders**: Puts burden on user to remind AI to remember

### Impact

**Affected Users**: All OpenClaw users using AI assistants for long-term tasks and personal assistance

**Severity**: High - affects trust and reliability of the AI assistant

**Frequency**: Always - every session end risks losing important information

**Consequences**:
- Trust issues when AI "forgets" promises
- Manual work to recover lost information
- Reduced reliability for commitment tracking
- Poor user experience in personal assistant scenarios

**Example**: User says "start evaluation framework tomorrow", AI doesn't save, next day user asks and AI has no record → broken trust

### Evidence/examples

**Related Documentation**:
- OpenClaw session management: https://docs.openclaw.ai
- Memory search feature: Already exists in OpenClaw (memory_search tool)
- Session history API: Already exists (sessions_history tool)

**Implementation Reference**:
- Similar to cron job system (already implemented)
- Could use existing MCP bridge infrastructure
- TypeScript implementation suggestion provided in description

### Additional information

_No response_

Last working version was 2026.4.12

items.title: plugin config apiKey field rejects boolean false — self-hosted plugins with no API key cannot suppress warning
items.body:
## Bug Description

When a plugin is configured to use local/bearer authentication (no API key required), setting `apiKey: false` in the plugin's `config` block is rejected by the config schema validation. The schema appears to require `apiKey` to be a string if present.

This prevents self-hosted plugins (like `openclaw-honcho` used with local auth) from properly suppressing the `HONCHO_API_KEY` warning.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Install `openclaw-honcho` plugin from clawhub.
2. Configure it with `baseUrl: http://localhost:8000` (local self-hosted instance, no API key).
3. Set `plugins.entries.openclaw-honcho.config.apiKey: false` to suppress the missing API key warning.
4. OpenClaw logs a warning or error about the config value type.

## Expected Behavior

The `apiKey` field in plugin config should accept:
- A string value (actual API key)
- `false` or `null` to explicitly indicate "no API key required"
- Omission of the field to mean "not configured"

## Environment

- openclaw version: 2026.4.15
- OS: Ubuntu/Linux
- Plugin: openclaw-honcho 1.2.2

## Impact

Self-hosted plugin users cannot suppress the `HONCHO_API_KEY` warning without modifying the plugin code. This creates noise in logs and suggests a misconfiguration when there is none.

## Additional Context

The Honcho plugin is configured to use a local server (`baseUrl: http://localhost:8000`) which does not use API key authentication. The plugin correctly detects this and logs a warning, but the only way to suppress it is to set `apiKey: false` — which is then rejected by schema validation.

items.title: subagent sessions left as "running" in sessions.json after crash — no cleanup mechanism
items.body:
## Bug Description

When a subagent process crashes, exits abnormally, or is killed without proper cleanup, its entry in `~/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json` is left with `status: "running"` indefinitely. The entry is never removed automatically.

This causes:
1. `sessions.json` to grow unbounded with stale entries
2. Any tool that reads `sessions.json` directly (scripts, external monitors) to see zombie "running" sessions
3. The `subagents` API (which uses an in-memory registry) returns empty, but `sessions.json` still shows the session as active

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Spawn a subagent via `sessions_spawn`.
2. Kill the subagent process forcefully (SIGKILL or host crash).
3. Check `sessions.json` — the entry remains with `status: "running"` and original `startedAt`.
4. The `subagents` tool API no longer shows this session, but it persists in `sessions.json`.

## Expected Behavior

OpenClaw should either:
- Clean up the session entry when the subagent process exits (even abnormally), or
- Implement a TTL/max-age mechanism to expire stale entries from `sessions.json`

## Environment

- openclaw version: 2026.4.15
- OS: Ubuntu/Linux
- Node.js process manager: systemd

## Impact

External monitoring scripts that read `sessions.json` directly see ghost sessions. Over time, `sessions.json` accumulates hundreds of stale entries.

## Additional Context

Workaround: manually edit `sessions.json` to remove orphaned entries. A cleanup script can identify ghosts using the pattern: `status="running" AND runtimeMs < 5min AND updatedAt > 30min ago`.

items.title: plugins.allow prevents bundled "subagents" CLI command from running
items.body:
## Bug Description

Setting `plugins.allow` to a list of plugin IDs (as required for some configurations) causes the `openclaw subagents` command to fail with:

```
Error: The openclaw subagents command is unavailable because plugins.allow excludes "subagents". Add "subagents" to plugins.allow if you want that bundled plugin CLI surface.
```

The `subagents` command is a built-in CLI surface, not a third-party plugin — it should not be gated by `plugins.allow`.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Set `plugins.allow` to any array that does NOT include "subagents" (even if all other plugins are allowlisted).
2. Run `openclaw subagents list` — it fails with the above error.
3. Workaround: manually add "subagents" to `plugins.allow`.

## Expected Behavior

Bundled CLI surfaces (like `openclaw subagents`) should be accessible regardless of `plugins.allow` configuration, or the error message should clarify that this is a built-in command not a plugin.

## Environment

- openclaw version: 2026.4.15
- OS: Ubuntu/Linux
- Config: `plugins.allow` set to array of plugin IDs

## Impact

Any user with a locked-down `plugins.allow` configuration cannot use the subagent management CLI.

## Additional Context

This was found when configuring `plugins.allow` for a production OpenClaw instance. The workaround of adding "subagents" to the allowlist works but is undocumented and confusing.

items.title: plugin: async register() return value is not awaited — routes never registered
items.body:
## Bug Description

When a plugin exports an async `register()` function, OpenClaw calls it synchronously without `await`. The returned Promise is silently dropped and the function's side effects (e.g. route registration) never execute.

## Steps to Reproduce

1. Create or use a plugin that exports `register()` as `async`.
2. Observe that routes/handlers registered inside `register()` are never mounted.
3. Check OpenClaw logs — no error is shown.

## Expected Behavior

OpenClaw should `await` the `register()` call, or at minimum warn if a Promise is returned and discarded.

## Environment

- openclaw version: 2026.4.15 (from openclaw --version)
- Installation method: npm global
- OS: Ubuntu/Linux
- Plugin: webhooks plugin (or any async register plugin)

## Impact

All plugins using async `register()` are silently broken — routes never mount. This affects at least the `webhooks` plugin.

## Additional Context

The warning "plugin register returned a promise; async registration is ignored" is logged but the register call itself is not awaited — the warning is descriptive of the problem but the underlying call is still synchronous.
